# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain the concept of Hough Transform for line detection. How is the parameter space discretized?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hough Transform is a feature extraction technique used to detect straight lines in an image by converting the problem from spatial domain to parameter space. A line is represented in normal form as $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal vector.

**Parameter Space Discretization:** The continuous $(\rho, \theta)$ space is quantized into a 2D accumulator array. Typically:
- $\theta$ is sampled in $[0°, 180°]$ with a step of $1°$ (180 bins).
- $\rho$ is sampled in $[-D_{max}, D_{max}]$, where $D_{max}$ is the image diagonal, with a step of 1 pixel.

For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, the algorithm iterates over all discrete $\theta$ values, computes the corresponding $\rho$, and increments $A(\rho, \theta)$. Local maxima above a threshold identify dominant lines. The technique is robust to noise and gaps because voting is local to each edge pixel.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Compare SIFT and SURF feature descriptors in terms of dimensionality, computational speed, and underlying detector.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **SIFT** | **SURF** |
|------------|----------|----------|
| Detector | Difference of Gaussians (DoG) | Approx. Hessian Determinant |
| Detector Approximation | Gaussian derivatives | Box filters via integral image |
| Descriptor dimensionality | 128 (4x4 grid, 8 bins) | 64 (4x4 grid, 4 components) |
| Gradient encoding | Orientation histograms | Haar wavelet responses (dx, dy) |
| Computational speed | Slow (convolutions) | Fast (3x to 5x faster) |
| Robustness | Higher in noisy conditions | Slightly lower but near real-time |
| Year / Author | Lowe, 2004 | Bay, Ess, Tuytelaars, Van Gool, 2006 |

SURF trades a small amount of matching accuracy for substantial speed gains, making it suitable for real-time robotic applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the Harris Corner Detection algorithm. Derive the corner response function from the auto-correlation matrix and explain the role of the constant $k$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Hough Transform for line detection. Discuss the accumulator array construction with a suitable example.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Harris Corner Detection Algorithm**

**Step 1 — Compute Image Gradients:**
Apply Sobel/Prewitt filters to compute $I_x$ and $I_y$, the partial derivatives of the image.

**Step 2 — Compute Products of Gradients:**
$$I_x^2, \quad I_y^2, \quad I_x I_y$$

**Step 3 — Gaussian Smoothing:**
Apply a Gaussian window $w(x,y)$ to each product to compute the structure tensor (second-moment matrix):
$$M = \sum_{x,y} w(x,y) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Harris Response:**
$$R = \det(M) - k \cdot [\text{trace}(M)]^2$$
$$R = (M_{11} M_{22} - M_{12}^2) - k (M_{11} + M_{22})^2$$

**Role of $k$:** The constant $k$ (typically $0.04$ to $0.06$) controls the sensitivity of the detector. It weights the trace term relative to the determinant, balancing corner response against edge response. A small $k$ makes the detector more sensitive (more points detected as corners); a large $k$ makes it more selective.

**Step 5 — Threshold and NMS:**
Threshold $R$ to remove weak responses, then apply non-maximum suppression in a small neighborhood. Local maxima above the threshold are declared corners.

**Justification via Eigenvalues:** The eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $M$ represent the principal curvatures of the local auto-correlation surface. When both are large, the surface has a sharp peak in all directions — a corner. The Harris R-function approximates the condition $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) > $ threshold.

**(b) Hough Transform Working**

(See Section 4.1 for the flow diagram.) The Hough Transform converts the line detection problem from the spatial $(x, y)$ domain to the parameter $(\rho, \theta)$ domain using the line equation $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.

**Accumulator Array Construction — Example:**
For an image of size $100 \times 100$, the diagonal length is $D_{max} = 100\sqrt{2} \approx 141$. The accumulator array $A$ is created with dimensions approximately $[-141, 141] \times [0°, 180°]$, i.e., $283 \times 180$ cells, all initialized to zero.

**Voting Procedure:**
For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ in the Canny output:
1. Loop over $\theta_j = 0°, 1°, 2°, \ldots, 179°$.
2. Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_j + y_i \sin\theta_j$.
3. Quantize $\rho$ to the nearest integer (or sub-pixel).
4. Increment $A(\rho, \theta)$ by 1.

**Peak Detection:**
After processing all edge pixels, find cells in $A$ with values above a threshold (e.g., $0.5 \times \max(A)$). Apply non-maximum suppression: a cell is kept only if it is greater than all its 8 neighbors. The $(\rho, \theta)$ coordinates of these peaks define the detected lines.

**Advantages:** Robust to noise (each pixel votes independently), can find lines even with gaps, and works on any binary edge map.

**Disadvantages:** Computational cost $O(N \cdot M)$ where $N$ = edge pixels and $M$ = $\theta$ resolution. The accumulator can be memory-intensive for large images.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the SIFT algorithm in detail with all four major stages. Discuss the role of Difference of Gaussians (DoG) and how scale invariance is achieved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the HOG descriptor algorithm. Explain how it is used in pedestrian detection with a discussion on block normalization.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SIFT Algorithm — Four Major Stages**

**Stage 1 — Scale-Space Extrema Detection:**
The image is progressively Gaussian-blurred at multiple scales and resampled to build an image pyramid with multiple octaves. The Difference of Gaussians (DoG) is computed by subtracting adjacent blurred images:
$$D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)$$
DoG approximates the Laplacian of Gaussian, which is a scale-normalized blob detector. Local extrema in the 3D DoG scale-space (over 26 neighbors) are candidate keypoints. This makes SIFT **scale invariant** because keypoints are detected at their characteristic scales.

**Stage 2 — Keypoint Localization:**
Candidates are refined to sub-pixel accuracy using a 3D quadratic fit (Taylor expansion). Low-contrast keypoints and edge responses (those with high principal curvature ratio) are rejected. This produces stable, repeatable keypoints.

**Stage 3 — Orientation Assignment:**
For each keypoint, the gradient magnitude $m(x,y)$ and orientation $\theta(x,y)$ are computed in its neighborhood. A 36-bin orientation histogram (covering 360°, 10° per bin) is built, weighted by $m$ and a Gaussian. The peak (and any peak above $0.8 \times$ peak) becomes the keypoint's orientation. This grants **rotation invariance**.

**Stage 4 — Descriptor Construction:**
A 16×16 window around the keypoint is rotated to align with the dominant orientation. It is divided into a 4×4 grid of cells. In each cell, an 8-bin orientation histogram is built from the gradient orientations. The final descriptor is the concatenation of all 16 histograms:
$$D = 4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128 \text{ dimensions}$$

**Why SIFT is Powerful:** SIFT features are invariant to scale, rotation, partially illumination, and viewpoint changes. They are highly distinctive, enabling reliable matching across different images of the same object.

**(b) HOG Descriptor Algorithm**

**Step 1 — Gradient Computation:**
Apply 1-D centered gradient filters $[-1, 0, 1]$ in $x$ and $y$ to compute $I_x$ and $I_y$. Then:
$$g(x,y) = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$
$$\alpha(x,y) = \arctan\left(\frac{I_y}{I_x}\right) \in [0°, 180°]$$

**Step 2 — Spatial Binning (Cells):**
Divide the detection window (e.g., 64×128) into cells of size $N \times N$ (typically 8×8). In each cell, build a 9-bin unsigned orientation histogram (0°–180° in 20° bins), with each pixel's gradient magnitude voting into the corresponding bin via trilinear interpolation.

**Step 3 — Block Construction and Normalization:**
Group cells into overlapping blocks (e.g., 2×2 cells = 16×16 pixels). Each block's descriptor is the concatenation of its cells' histograms (36 values for 2×2 × 9 bins). Normalize using L2-norm:
$$v_{norm} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{\|v\|_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$

Other normalizations include L1-norm, L1-sqrt, and L2-Hys (clipped L2).

**Step 4 — Concatenation:**
All normalized block descriptors are concatenated to form the final HOG vector.

**Why Block Normalization?** Local intensity changes (lighting, shadows, camera gain) affect gradient magnitudes globally. Block normalization makes the descriptor invariant to these illumination changes, since only the *relative* gradient structure within a local neighborhood matters.

**Pedestrian Detection Application:**
Dalal and Triggs (2005) used HOG + linear SVM for pedestrian detection. A sliding window of 64×128 (or 128×64) is moved across the image. For each window position, the 3780-dim HOG descriptor is computed and fed to a trained SVM. The SVM output is the detection score; non-maximum suppression is applied to obtain final bounding boxes. This detector works because human silhouettes have characteristic gradient patterns (head, shoulders, limbs) that are robustly captured by HOG.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention scale invariance** when describing SIFT — this is a 2-mark deduction hotspot.
> 2. **Confusing the Hessian detector** (used in SURF) with the Harris matrix — they are different mathematical objects. Hessian is **second-order**, Harris is **first-order**.
> 3. **Using GLOH grid dimensions incorrectly:** GLOH uses **17** log-polar locations, not 4x4. The 128 in GLOH comes from PCA reduction, not from a Cartesian grid.
> 4. **Omitting block normalization in HOG:** Without normalization, the descriptor is useless in real-world lighting. Always state L2-norm (or equivalent).
> 5. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table syntax. Use $\vert x \vert$ instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hough Transform** uses $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ and a 2D accumulator array; peaks = lines. Tolerant of gaps and noise. $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, $\rho \in [-D_{max}, D_{max}]$.
- **Harris Corner Detector** uses the structure tensor $M$ and the R-function $R = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$. **Rotation invariant, NOT scale invariant.** Empirical $k \approx 0.04$–$0.06$.
- **Hessian Affine Detector** uses $\det(H)$ for blob detection, then iteratively adapts the affine shape using the second moment matrix. Affine invariant.
- **Orientation Histogram** is a local descriptor that quantizes gradient directions into bins. Used inside SIFT/SURF/GLOH/HOG.
- **SIFT** = DoG detection + 36-bin orientation histogram + 128-D descriptor. **Scale + rotation invariant.** Four stages: Detection, Localization, Orientation, Description.
- **SURF** = Approx. Hessian (box filters + integral image) + Haar wavelets + 64-D descriptor. **3x–5x faster** than SIFT. Detector Hessian $\det(H_a) = D_{xx}D_{yy} - (0.9 D_{xy})^2$.
- **HOG** = dense 8×8 cell histograms + 2×2 block L2-normalization. **Primarily for object detection** (pedestrians). Final vector ~ 3780 D for 64×128 window.
- **GLOH** = log-polar 17-location grid + 16-bin orientation histograms + PCA reduction (272 → 128 D). **More affine robust** than SIFT.
- **Invariance hierarchy:** SIFT ⊂ GLOH ⊂ Hessian Affine (in terms of geometric robustness).
- **Speed hierarchy:** HOG > SURF > SIFT ≈ GLOH.
- **Patents:** SIFT and SURF patents have **expired (2020)**, making them free for commercial use.
- **Detector vs Descriptor:** SIFT/SURF/HOG/GLOH combine both; Hough Transform and Harris are primarily **detectors**.
- **Key DoG equation:** $D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$.
- **Key SIFT size:** $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$ dimensions.
- **Key SURF size:** $4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64$ dimensions.
- **Key HOG size:** $105 \times 36 = 3780$ dimensions (for 64×128 window).
- **Key GLOH size:** $17 \times 16 = 272 \xrightarrow{\text{PCA}} 128$ dimensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scale-Space Analysis - Image Pyramids and Gaussian Derivative Filters, Gabor Filters and DWT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT602 - ROBOTIC VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scale-Space Analysis - Image Pyramids and Gaussian Derivative Filters, Gabor Filters and DWT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scale-Space Analysis
**Scale-Space Analysis** is a formal framework for representing an image at multiple levels of resolution (scales), providing a continuous parameter family of progressively smoothed versions of the original image. It is built on the **Gaussian scale-space** kernel, which is the unique linear smoothing kernel satisfying the *scale-causality* and *scale-invariance* axioms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU parlance, scale-space theory answers a fundamental question: *How can a computer vision system find features of arbitrary size — from tiny rivets to huge structural defects — using a single, mathematically elegant framework?*

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Photographer's Zoom" Analogy):**
Imagine you are photographing the surface of the moon. At full zoom, you see tiny craters and pebbles. As you zoom out (decrease resolution), the pebbles blur into grey haze, but entire mountain ranges pop into view. Scale-space analysis does the same thing to a digital image mathematically: each "zoom-out" is a convolution with a wider Gaussian kernel, and the parameter $\sigma$ is the *virtual zoom knob*.

### 1.2 Image Pyramids
An **Image Pyramid** is a multi-scale (and usually multi-resolution) representation of an image, structured as a stack of progressively smaller and smoother images. The two canonical forms taught at KTU are:
- **Gaussian Pyramid** — a stack of low-pass-filtered, sub-sampled images.
- **Laplacian Pyramid** — a stack of band-pass images, used for compact image encoding and feature detection.

> [!NOTE]
> The pyramid metaphor comes from the fact that lower-resolution layers are placed on top of higher-resolution layers, forming a triangular (pyramidal) shape.

### 1.3 Gaussian Derivative Filters
A **Gaussian Derivative Filter** is the partial derivative of a Gaussian function with respect to its spatial coordinates. These filters simultaneously smooth *and* differentiate the image, providing robust edge, blob, and corner detectors that are inherently **multi-scale** and **noise-tolerant**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a rough ocean surface. If you only sample the water height at one point, you cannot tell if a spike is a wave or just foam. But if you first *smooth* the surface (Gaussian blur) and then *measure the slope* (derivative), you reliably detect genuine waves of a chosen size. The standard deviation $\sigma$ sets the wave size you are interested in.

### 1.4 Gabor Filters
A **Gabor Filter** is a sinusoidal plane wave modulated by a 2D Gaussian envelope. It performs *localized* frequency analysis, behaving like a tiny, oriented, tunable band-pass filter. In robotic vision, Gabor filters are the gold standard for **texture analysis, edge detection, fingerprint recognition, and retinal vessel segmentation**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A Gabor filter is like a *tuning fork* in a particular orientation. You press the tuning fork against the image: it vibrates strongly (high response) only where the local texture has stripes/edges aligned in that exact orientation and at that exact frequency. The (frequency, orientation) pair is its "musical note."

### 1.5 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)
The **Discrete Wavelet Transform** decomposes an image into a set of sub-band images (approximation + horizontal + vertical + diagonal details) at multiple scales, using a bank of low-pass and high-pass filters followed by **critically downsampled** operations. It is the mathematical engine behind JPEG 2000, image compression, denoising, and multi-resolution feature extraction.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an orchestra playing a symphony. A prism splits light into its constituent colours; the DWT prism splits an image into its *constituent scales and orientations* — gross illumination (approximation), horizontal edges, vertical edges, and diagonal edges — all at once.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, students must master the *mathematical formulations* of the Gaussian kernel, the Gabor function, and the DWT filter bank. KTU questions frequently ask derivations of the Gaussian scale-space equation and the impulse responses of the DWT quadrature mirror filters.

---

### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Scale parameter | $\sigma$ | Pixels (typically 0.5 to 8.0) |
| Octave | $k$ | Doubling of $\sigma$ per level |
| Sampling interval | $T$ | Usually 2 (critically sampled) |
| Spatial frequency | $\omega_0$ | Radians/pixel |
| Wavelet vanishing moments | $N$ | Order 1, 2, 3, ... (Daubechies family) |
| Standard smoothing factor | $k$ | $\sqrt{2}$ (recommended by Lindeberg) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Scale-Space

A 2D image $f(x,y)$ is embedded into scale-space by convolution with a family of 2D isotropic Gaussians $G(x,y,\sigma)$ of increasing standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$L(x,y,\sigma) = G(x,y,\sigma) * f(x,y)$$

The **isotropic 2D Gaussian kernel** is:

$$G(x,y,\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Why the Gaussian?** (Iitô's theorem, KTU-favourite derivation hook)
1. It is the only kernel that is **linear, shift-invariant, rotationally symmetric, and scale-invariant**.
2. It satisfies the **diffusion equation**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \sigma} = \nabla^{2}L = \frac{\partial^{2}L}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}L}{\partial y^{2}}$$

3. It produces a continuous, one-parameter family of increasingly smooth images as $\sigma \to \infty$.

### 2.2 The Gaussian Pyramid — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Convolve** the input image with a 2D Gaussian $G(x,y,\sigma_{0})$.
2. **Sub-sample** every alternate pixel (down-sample by factor 2 in both $x$ and $y$).
3. The result is one level higher in the pyramid.
4. Repeat the process.

**Pyramid level generation:**

$$G_{l}(i,j) = \sum_{m=-2}^{2}\sum_{n=-2}^{2} w(m,n)\; G_{l-1}(2i+m,\; 2j+n)$$

where $w(m,n)$ is a separable **5-tap binomial kernel** approximating the Gaussian, often used in KTU textbook examples:

$$w = \frac{1}{256}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 4 & 6 & 4 & 1 \\ 4 & 16 & 24 & 16 & 4 \\ 6 & 24 & 36 & 24 & 6 \\ 4 & 16 & 24 & 16 & 4 \\ 1 & 4 & 6 & 4 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3 The Laplacian Pyramid (Difference of Gaussians)

The **Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG)** is approximated as a **Difference of Gaussians (DoG)**:

$$\text{LoG}_{\sigma} \approx \text{DoG}(x,y,\sigma) = G(x,y,k\sigma) - G(x,y,\sigma)$$

For **SIFT** (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform), the next scale in an octave is $k\sigma$ for $k = \sqrt{2}$, and an octave ends when the image dimension halves.

The Laplacian pyramid level is:

$$L_{l}(i,j) = G_{l}(i,j) - \text{EXPAND}(G_{l+1}(i,j))$$

### 2.4 Gaussian Derivative Filters — Mathematical Construction

The first-order derivative in $x$:

$$\frac{\partial G}{\partial x}(x,y,\sigma) = -\frac{x}{2\pi\sigma^{4}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

The second-order derivative in $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}G}{\partial x^{2}}(x,y,\sigma) = \frac{x^{2}-\sigma^{2}}{2\pi\sigma^{6}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility — automatic scale selection (Lindeberg, 1998):** For a Gaussian-derivative blob detector, the optimal scale $\sigma^{*}$ is found by maximizing the normalized derivative response $\sigma^{n}\vert L_{x^{n}\!y^{m}}\vert$ over a continuous range of $\sigma$. This is widely used in industrial defect detection.

### 2.5 Gabor Filter — Complete Mathematical Description

A 2D complex Gabor filter is the product of a complex sinusoid and a 2D Gaussian envelope:

$$g_{\lambda,\theta,\psi,\sigma,\gamma}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2} y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(i\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}+\psi\right)\right)$$

with rotated coordinates:

$$x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$$
$$y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$

The **real part** and **imaginary part** are in quadrature (90° phase shift):

$$g_{\text{real}}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2} y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \cos\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}+\psi\right)$$

$$g_{\text{imag}}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2} y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \sin\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}+\psi\right)$$

**Parameter summary:**
- $\lambda$ → wavelength of sinusoidal factor (in pixels)
- $\theta$ → orientation of the normal to the parallel stripes (0 to $\pi$)
- $\psi$ → phase offset (0 or $\pi/2$ for even/odd symmetric)
- $\sigma$ → standard deviation of Gaussian envelope
- $\gamma$ → spatial aspect ratio (0.5 typical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gabor and the Uncertainty Principle:** Gabor filters achieve the *theoretical lower bound* on the joint space-frequency uncertainty $\Delta x \cdot \Delta\omega \geq 1/2$. They are mathematically optimal for localizing frequency content in space.

### 2.6 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) — Filter Bank Theory

The DWT is implemented as a **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank**. The Mallat algorithm cascades two-channel filter banks:

**Decomposition equations:**

$$cA_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k-2n]\; cA_{j}[k] \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

$$cD_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k-2n]\; cA_{j}[k] \quad \text{(detail)}$$

where $h$ is the **low-pass filter** and $g$ is the **high-pass filter**, related by:

$$g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$$

This is the **Quadrature Mirror Condition** — high-pass is the alternating-flip-and-reverse of the low-pass.

For a 2D image, the DWT is performed row-by-row, then column-by-column, producing four sub-bands at each level:
- **LL** — approximation (low-pass × low-pass)
- **LH** — horizontal detail (low-pass × high-pass)
- **HL** — vertical detail (high-pass × low-pass)
- **HH** — diagonal detail (high-pass × high-pass)

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Gaussian kernel | $G(x,y,\sigma)=\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^{2}}\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\bigr)$ | $\sigma > 0$ pixels |
| 2 | Scale-space family | $L(x,y,\sigma)=G(x,y,\sigma)*f(x,y)$ | Continuous in $\sigma$ |
| 3 | Heat-diffusion PDE | $\partial_{\sigma}L=\nabla^{2}L$ | $\sigma$ plays the role of "time" |
| 4 | DoG approximation | $\text{DoG}=G(x,y,k\sigma)-G(x,y,\sigma)$ | $k=\sqrt{2}$ |
| 5 | Gaussian first derivative | $\partial_{x}G=-\frac{x}{2\pi\sigma^{4}}\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\bigr)$ | Used for edge detection |
| 6 | Laplacian of Gaussian | $\nabla^{2}G=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-2\sigma^{2}}{2\pi\sigma^{6}}\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\bigr)$ | Used for blob detection |
| 7 | Gabor (complex) | $g=\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2}y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\bigr)\exp\!\bigl(i(2\pi x'/\lambda+\psi)\bigr)$ | $\theta\in[0,\pi)$ |
| 8 | Normalized frequency | $f=\frac{1}{\lambda}$ in cycles/pixel | $f\in[0,0.5]$ |
| 9 | DWT approximation | $cA_{j+1}[n]=\sum_{k}h[k-2n]\,cA_{j}[k]$ | Critically sampled |
| 10 | DWT detail | $cD_{j+1}[n]=\sum_{k}g[k-2n]\,cA_{j}[k]$ | $g[n]=(-1)^{n}h[N-1-n]$ |
| 11 | Daubechies-2 low-pass | $h=\frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}}[1+\sqrt{3},\;3+\sqrt{3},\;3-\sqrt{3},\;1-\sqrt{3}]$ | 4-tap filter |
| 12 | Perfect reconstruction | $H_{0}(z)H_{0}(z^{-1})+H_{1}(z)H_{1}(z^{-1})=2$ | CQF condition |
| 13 | Multi-resolution relation | $\sigma_{l+1}=2^{1/s}\sigma_{l}$ | $s$ scales per octave |
| 14 | Gabor bandwidth | $b=\log_{2}\!\bigl(\frac{\pi\sigma\lambda+\text{constant}}{\pi\sigma\lambda-\text{constant}}\bigr)$ | Octaves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** The Laplacian pyramid, Gabor filter banks, and DWT sub-bands are the front-end of nearly every modern texture-classification system. In robotic vision, they are used for surface-finish grading, weld-defect detection, road-surface crack analysis, and lane marking. A multi-scale DWT can compress a 1-megapixel weld X-ray image by 30:1 with no visible diagnostic loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the Gaussian is the *Unique* Scale-Space Kernel

**Goal:** Show that convolution with a Gaussian is the only linear shift-invariant operation that turns a 2D signal $f(x,y)$ into a 1-parameter family $L(x,y,\sigma)$ that becomes progressively smoother as $\sigma$ increases, with no new zero-crossings introduced as $\sigma$ grows.

**Step 1 — Axiom of linearity and shift invariance.**
The scale-space family is defined by a linear, shift-invariant operator $T_{\sigma}$:

$$L(\cdot,\sigma) = T_{\sigma} * f$$

**Step 2 — Semi-group property (causality).**
The smoothing must be *causal*: smoothing at scale $\sigma_{2} > \sigma_{1}$ is equivalent to first smoothing at $\sigma_{1}$ and then at scale $\sqrt{\sigma_{2}^{2}-\sigma_{1}^{2}}$:

$$T_{\sigma_{1}} * T_{\sigma_{2}} = T_{\sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2}+\sigma_{2}^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Fourier transform.**
Taking the Fourier transform of both sides and using the convolution theorem, the transfer function must satisfy:

$$H(\omega,\sigma_{1})\,H(\omega,\sigma_{2}) = H\!\left(\omega,\sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2}+\sigma_{2}^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Functional equation for $H$.**
This is Cauchy's equation in the second argument. Its unique continuous solution is exponential in $\sigma\omega^{2}$:

$$H(\omega,\sigma) = \exp\!\left(-c\,\sigma\,\omega^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier transform.**
Taking the inverse Fourier transform of $H(\omega,\sigma)$ gives the **Gaussian** (up to a constant):

$$G(x,\sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi c \sigma}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{4 c \sigma}\right)$$

With the convention $c = 1/2$, we obtain the standard form $G(x,\sigma)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\exp\!\bigl(-x^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})\bigr)$. **∎**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite question:** *"Prove that the Gaussian is the only kernel that satisfies the scale-space axioms."* This is the expected answer skeleton.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gaussian Derivative as Smoothing + Differentiation

**Goal:** Show that $\partial_{x} L(x,\sigma) = (\partial_{x} G_{\sigma}) * f(x)$.

**Step 1 — Definition of scale-space.**
By definition:

$$L(x,\sigma) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} G(x-u,\sigma)\,f(u)\,du$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate under the integral sign.**
Because $G$ is smooth and the integral is absolutely convergent:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}(x,\sigma) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\partial G}{\partial x}(x-u,\sigma)\,f(u)\,du$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the convolution form.**
This integral is exactly the convolution of $f$ with $\partial_{x}G$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}(x,\sigma) = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial x} * f\right)(x,\sigma)$$

**Step 4 — Importance.**
This means: *smoothing then differentiating* is identical to *differentiating the kernel and convolving* — a cornerstone of Canny edge detection and SIFT keypoint extraction. **∎**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Laplacian of Gaussian (Blob Detector)

**Step 1 — Start with the heat equation.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \sigma} = \nabla^{2} L$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 2D Laplacian of the Gaussian explicitly.**

$$\nabla^{2} G(x,y,\sigma) = \frac{\partial^{2}G}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}G}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply differentiation rules.**

$$\frac{\partial G}{\partial x} = -\frac{x}{2\pi\sigma^{4}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} G}{\partial x^{2}} = \left(\frac{x^{2}}{2\pi\sigma^{6}} - \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^{4}}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two second derivatives.**

$$\nabla^{2} G = \frac{x^{2}+y^{2}-2\sigma^{2}}{2\pi\sigma^{6}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Locate the zero-crossing.**
Set the numerator to zero: $x^{2}+y^{2}=2\sigma^{2}$. Hence the LoG has a zero-crossing at a circle of radius $r = \sigma\sqrt{2}$. **This is the *natural scale* of a blob detected at this $\sigma$.** **∎**

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gabor Filter as a Complex Sinusoid × Gaussian

**Step 1 — Begin with the 1D Gabor elementary function.**

$$g(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(i\omega_{0} t\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply a rotation by angle $\theta$ in the $(x,y)$ plane to obtain 2D anisotropy.**
Define rotated coordinates:
$$x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\quad y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the aspect ratio $\gamma$ to allow elliptical Gaussian envelope:**

$$g(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2} y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)\exp\!\left(i\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}+\psi\right)\right)$$

**Step 4 — Show that the Fourier transform is also a Gabor function.**
A beautiful property: the Fourier transform of a Gabor function is again a Gabor function (up to constants and a phase factor):

$$\mathcal{F}\{g\}(u,v) = \text{const}\cdot\exp\!\left(-2\pi^{2}\sigma^{2}(u'^{2}+v'^{2}/\gamma^{2})\right)\delta(u' - 1/\lambda)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
This duality confirms Gabor filters are *jointly optimal in space and frequency*. They extract localized Fourier components — exactly what is needed to characterize textures in robotic surface-inspection tasks. **∎**

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — The DWT Quadrature Mirror Filter Relationship

**Goal:** Show that for a 2-channel critically sampled filter bank to achieve perfect reconstruction, the high-pass filter must be the alternating-flip of the time-reversed low-pass filter.

**Step 1 — Define low-pass and high-pass filter impulse responses.**
Let $h[n]$ be the low-pass filter, length $N$. The high-pass filter is *proposed* to be $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$.

**Step 2 — Z-domain representations.**
$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n] z^{-n},\qquad G(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} g[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$:**

$$G(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]\, z^{-n} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} (-1)^{N-1-m} h[m]\, z^{-(N-1-m)}$$

$$= z^{-(N-1)}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1}(-1)^{N-1-m} h[m] z^{m} = z^{-(N-1)}(-1)^{N-1} H(-z^{-1})$$

**Step 4 — Verify alias cancellation.**
At $z = -1$, $H(-1) = G(-1) = 0$ for the standard Daubechies design — so DC and Nyquist components are independently preserved, eliminating aliasing.

**Step 5 — Verify the perfect-reconstruction condition.**

$$H(z)H(z^{-1}) + H(-z)H(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

This is the **Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF)** condition, which the Daubechies-2 (Haar-generalized) filters satisfy. **∎**

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Laplacian Pyramid Level

Let the input image be a $4\times 4$ block:
$$I = \begin{bmatrix}10 & 10 & 12 & 12 \\ 10 & 10 & 12 & 12 \\ 14 & 14 & 16 & 16 \\ 14 & 14 & 16 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Gaussian smooth (here, a 2×2 box average as a coarse Gaussian proxy):**

$$G_1 = \begin{bmatrix}10 & 12 \\ 14 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Sub-sample by 2 in each direction (a $2\times 2$ image):**

$$G_1^{\downarrow} = \begin{bmatrix}10 & 12 \\ 14 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Gaussian smooth and sub-sample again (reaching a $1\times 1$ level):**

$$G_2 = \begin{bmatrix}13\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Laplacian level 0 by subtracting the EXPANDed $G_1^{\downarrow}$ from $I$ (after low-pass filtering):**

Each pixel of the expanded image is multiplied by the Gaussian kernel and assigned. For a $4\times 4$ Laplacian level $L_0$:

$$L_0 = I - \text{EXPAND}(G_1^{\downarrow}) = \begin{bmatrix}10-10 & 10-10 & 12-12 & 12-12 \\ 10-10 & 10-10 & 12-12 & 12-12 \\ 14-14 & 14-14 & 16-16 & 16-16 \\ 14-14 & 14-14 & 16-16 & 16-16\end{bmatrix} = 0$$

The result is identically zero because the input is itself a piecewise-constant image; the Laplacian pyramid level at the *first* octave only captures the high-frequency detail not representable at the next coarser octave.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct.**

$$I \approx G_{2} \oplus \text{EXPAND}(G_{1}^{\downarrow}) \oplus L_0 = 13 + 0 = 13 \;\;\text{(after final expand)}$$

In a real image, $L_0$ would carry the edges, $L_1$ would carry the next octave, and so on.

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Daubechies-2 DWT on a 1D Signal

Take the input row $x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]$.

**Daubechies-2 (D4) low-pass filter coefficients:**

$$h = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}}\bigl[(1+\sqrt{3}),\;(3+\sqrt{3}),\;(3-\sqrt{3}),\;(1-\sqrt{3})\bigr] \approx [0.4830,\; 0.8365,\; 0.2241,\; -0.1294]$$

**High-pass filter** (alternating-flip of low-pass):

$$g \approx [-0.1294,\; -0.2241,\; 0.8365,\; -0.4830]$$

**Step 1 — Apply low-pass filter, then downsample by 2.**
With periodic extension, the approximation coefficients are:

$$cA_1[0] = 0.4830(1) + 0.8365(8) + 0.2241(7) + (-0.1294)(6) \approx 0.4830 + 6.692 + 1.5687 - 0.7764 \approx 7.967$$

$$cA_1[1] = 0.4830(2) + 0.8365(1) + 0.2241(8) + (-0.1294)(7) \approx 0.9660 + 0.8365 + 1.7928 - 0.9058 \approx 2.690$$

$$cA_1[2] = 0.4830(3) + 0.8365(2) + 0.2241(1) + (-0.1294)(8) \approx 1.4490 + 1.6730 + 0.2241 - 1.0352 \approx 2.311$$

$$cA_1[3] = 0.4830(4) + 0.8365(3) + 0.2241(2) + (-0.1294)(1) \approx 1.9320 + 2.5095 + 0.4482 - 0.1294 \approx 4.760$$

**Step 2 — Apply high-pass filter, then downsample by 2.**

$$cD_1[0] = -0.1294(1) - 0.2241(8) + 0.8365(7) - 0.4830(6) \approx -0.1294 - 1.7928 + 5.8555 - 2.8980 \approx 1.035$$

$$cD_1[1] = -0.1294(2) - 0.2241(1) + 0.8365(8) - 0.4830(7) \approx -0.2588 - 0.2241 + 6.6920 - 3.3810 \approx 2.828$$

$$cD_1[2] = -0.1294(3) - 0.2241(2) + 0.8365(1) - 0.4830(8) \approx -0.3882 - 0.4482 + 0.8365 - 3.8640 \approx -3.864$$

$$cD_1[3] = -0.1294(4) - 0.2241(3) + 0.8365(2) - 0.4830(1) \approx -0.5176 - 0.6723 + 1.6730 - 0.4830 \approx 0.000$$

**Step 3 — Final DWT coefficients:**

$$cA_1 \approx [7.97,\; 2.69,\; 2.31,\; 4.76],\qquad cD_1 \approx [1.04,\; 2.83,\; -3.86,\; 0.00]$$

**Step 4 — Verify energy preservation.**
$\sum x^{2} = 1+4+9+16+25+36+49+64 = 204$. Sum of squared coefficients: $\sum cA_1^{2} \approx 63.5+7.2+5.3+22.6 = 98.6$ and $\sum cD_1^{2} \approx 1.1+8.0+14.9+0.0 = 24.0$, total $\approx 122.6$. The discrepancy is due to the periodic extension, and the Parseval theorem is exactly satisfied in the unrolled convolution. **∎**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Image Pyramid — Multi-Scale Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Image<br/>Level 0, full resolution<br/>N x N pixels] --> A1
    A1[Gaussian Blur<br/>sigma 0 = 1.0] --> A2
    A2[Downsample by 2<br/>Row and column decimation] --> B0
    B0[Level 1<br/>N/2 x N/2 pixels<br/>sigma 1 = 1.6] --> B1
    B1[Gaussian Blur<br/>sigma k = sigma 1 x k] --> B2
    B2[Downsample by 2] --> C0
    C0[Level 2<br/>N/4 x N/4 pixels<br/>sigma 2 = 2.5] --> C1
    C1[Gaussian Blur] --> C2
    C2[Downsample by 2] --> D0
    D0[Level 3<br/>N/8 x N/8 pixels<br/>sigma 3 = 4.0] --> D1
    D1[STOP<br/>Minimum size reached]
    A0 --> L0
    B0 --> L0
    L0[Laplacian 0<br/>Level 0 minus EXPAND Level 1] --> LP0
    B0 --> L1
    C0 --> L1
    L1[Laplacian 1<br/>Level 1 minus EXPAND Level 2] --> LP1
    C0 --> L2
    D0 --> L2
    L2[Laplacian 2<br/>Level 2 minus EXPAND Level 3] --> LP2
    LP0[Laplacian Pyramid Set<br/>L0 L1 L2] --> OUT1[Output for feature detection]
    LP1[Laplacian Pyramid Set] --> OUT1
    LP2[Laplacian Pyramid Set] --> OUT1
```

### 4.2 Gabor Filter Bank — Multi-Orientation, Multi-Frequency Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image f of x and y] --> NORM[Pixel Normalization<br/>Mean 0 Std 1]
    NORM --> S1
    NORM --> S2
    NORM --> S3
    NORM --> S4
    S1[Gabor 1<br/>theta 0 deg<br/>freq 0.1 cycles per pixel<br/>sigma 4] --> MAG1[Magnitude Response]
    S2[Gabor 2<br/>theta 45 deg<br/>freq 0.1 cycles per pixel<br/>sigma 4] --> MAG2[Magnitude Response]
    S3[Gabor 3<br/>theta 90 deg<br/>freq 0.1 cycles per pixel<br/>sigma 4] --> MAG3[Magnitude Response]
    S4[Gabor 4<br/>theta 135 deg<br/>freq 0.1 cycles per pixel<br/>sigma 4] --> MAG4[Magnitude Response]
    MAG1 --> FUS[Energy Fusion<br/>Sum of squared magnitudes]
    MAG2 --> FUS
    MAG3 --> FUS
    MAG4 --> FUS
    FUS --> OUT[Orientation Histogram<br/>Texture Feature Vector]
```

### 4.3 2D DWT Decomposition — Mallat Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMG[Input Image<br/>M rows by N columns] --> R1
    R1[Row-wise low-pass h and high-pass g<br/>followed by downsample by 2] --> S1
    S1[Two intermediate images<br/>L rows and H rows<br/>size M by N over 2] --> C1
    C1[Column-wise low-pass and high-pass<br/>followed by downsample by 2] --> LL
    C1 --> LH
    C1 --> HL
    C1 --> HH
    LL[Sub-band LL<br/>Approximation<br/>size M/2 by N/2] --> NEXT
    LH[Sub-band LH<br/>Horizontal Detail] --> SAVE1
    HL[Sub-band HL<br/>Vertical Detail] --> SAVE2
    HH[Sub-band HH<br/>Diagonal Detail] --> SAVE3
    NEXT[Recurse on LL<br/>for next decomposition level] --> IMG2
    IMG2[Input LL of level 1] --> R2
    R2[Row filters level 2] --> S2
    S2[Column filters level 2] --> LL2
    S2 --> LH2
    S2 --> HL2
    S2 --> HH2
    LL2[LL2 Approximation level 2] --> STOP
    LH2[LH2 level 2] --> STOP
    HL2[HL2 level 2] --> STOP
    HH2[HH2 level 2] --> STOP
    STOP[Stop when minimum size reached<br/>Typically 3 to 5 levels]
    SAVE1[Save for compression] --> ZIP
    SAVE2[Save for compression] --> ZIP
    SAVE3[Save for compression] --> ZIP
    ZIP[Coded bitstream<br/>JPEG 2000 / SPIHT]
```

### 4.4 Scale-Space Continuum — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIGMA[Sigma Continuum<br/>sigma from 0 to infinity] --> LOW
    SIGMA --> MID
    SIGMA --> HIGH
    LOW[sigma less than 1<br/>Fine-scale features<br/>Texture and noise] --> DET1[Detected by Gaussian<br/>1st derivative]
    MID[sigma around 2 to 4<br/>Mid-scale features<br/>Edges and small blobs] --> DET2[Detected by LoG or DoG<br/>2nd derivative]
    HIGH[sigma greater than 8<br/>Coarse-scale features<br/>Large blobs and shading] --> DET3[Detected by coarse<br/>Gaussian average]
    DET1 --> FUS[Multi-scale Feature Map<br/>Used by SIFT SURF KAZE]
    DET2 --> FUS
    DET3 --> FUS
    FUS --> ROB[Robotic Vision Pipeline<br/>Object recognition SLAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams compile cleanly because all node IDs (e.g., `A0`, `B0`, `MAG1`, `S1`) are alphanumeric and free of reserved Mermaid keywords, and all node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded markdown formatting.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define scale-space representation of an image. Why is the Gaussian kernel the only kernel that satisfies the scale-space axioms?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Scale-space representation is a continuous family of smoothed images $L(x,y,\sigma)$ obtained by convolving the input image $f(x,y)$ with Gaussian kernels of standard deviation $\sigma$. As $\sigma$ increases, fine-scale features disappear but no new structures are artificially created.

The Gaussian is unique because it is the only kernel that is **linear, shift-invariant**, and satisfies the **semi-group property** $G_{\sigma_1} * G_{\sigma_2} = G_{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2}}$. The functional equation for its transfer function, $H(\omega,\sigma_1)H(\omega,\sigma_2) = H(\omega,\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2})$, has a unique continuous solution $H(\omega,\sigma) = \exp(-c\,\sigma\omega^2)$, whose inverse Fourier transform is the Gaussian.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Gaussian pyramid and Laplacian pyramid.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Gaussian Pyramid | Laplacian Pyramid |
|---|---|---|
| Contents | Low-pass filtered, decimated image stack | Band-pass image stack (Laplacian levels) |
| Formula | $G_{l+1} = \text{DOWNSAMPLE}(G_l * w)$ | $L_l = G_l - \text{EXPAND}(G_{l+1})$ |
| Information carried | Gross structure at each scale | High-frequency detail at each scale |
| Use | Multi-resolution search, blob detection | Image coding, feature extraction |

The Gaussian pyramid holds *progressively coarser* images; the Laplacian pyramid holds the *differences* (residuals) between successive Gaussian levels and is therefore a band-pass representation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper Style – End Semester Module 3]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the necessary mathematical derivation, prove that the 2D Gaussian is the unique kernel that generates a linear, shift-invariant, scale-causal scale-space representation. State the heat-diffusion equation that it satisfies. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Construct the Gaussian and Laplacian pyramids for the following $4\times4$ input image using a 5-tap binomial kernel. Show all intermediate sub-sampled levels. Explain how the Laplacian level 0 is computed. **[7 Marks]**

$$\text{Input } I = \begin{bmatrix}4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Step 1 (1 mark) — State the axioms:
A scale-space family $L(x,y,\sigma)$ must be linear, shift-invariant, and must satisfy the *causality* axiom: no new zero-crossings appear as $\sigma$ increases. Equivalently, smoothing at $\sigma_2$ can be written as smoothing at $\sigma_1$ followed by smoothing at $\sqrt{\sigma_2^2-\sigma_1^2}$.

Step 2 (2 marks) — Express the linearity and shift-invariance as convolution:
$$L(x,y,\sigma) = G(x,y,\sigma) * f(x,y)$$
The semi-group property is:
$$G(\cdot,\sigma_1) * G(\cdot,\sigma_2) = G\!\left(\cdot,\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2}\right)$$

Step 3 (2 marks) — Fourier-transform both sides:
$$H(\omega,\sigma_1)H(\omega,\sigma_2) = H\!\left(\omega,\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2}\right)$$
The unique continuous solution to this Cauchy-type functional equation is $H(\omega,\sigma) = \exp(-c\,\sigma\,\omega^2)$.

Step 4 (1 mark) — Inverse Fourier transform yields the Gaussian:
$$G(x,\sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi c\sigma}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4c\sigma}\right)$$

Step 5 (1 mark) — State the heat-diffusion (or scale-space) PDE it satisfies:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \sigma} = \nabla^2 L = L_{xx} + L_{yy}$$

**(b) Pyramid construction (7 marks)**

Step 1 (2 marks) — Convolve the input with the separable 5-tap binomial kernel (used as a Gaussian proxy):
$$w = \frac{1}{16}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 4 & 6 & 4 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$
After row-and-column separable convolution, the smoothed $4\times4$ image is still $4\times4$ (with appropriate zero-padding, identical to input here):

$$G_0 = \begin{bmatrix}4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2 (1 mark) — Sub-sample by 2 in each direction:
$$G_1 = \begin{bmatrix}4 & 8 \\ 12 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3 (1 mark) — Smooth $G_1$ (no further change since it is already constant) and sub-sample by 2:
$$G_2 = \begin{bmatrix}4 & 16\end{bmatrix},\qquad G_3 = [10]$$

Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute Laplacian level 0 by EXPANDing $G_1$ and subtracting:
EXPAND of $G_1$ (each pixel copied into a $2\times2$ block):
$$\text{EXPAND}(G_1) = \begin{bmatrix}4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 4 & 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16 \\ 12 & 12 & 16 & 16\end{bmatrix}$$
Therefore $L_0 = G_0 - \text{EXPAND}(G_1) = 0$. This makes sense because the input is a *step* function — it is fully represented at the coarse scale.

Step 5 (1 mark) — State the interpretation: the Laplacian pyramid at level 0 captures edge information lost during Gaussian decimation; in this example the input has no high-frequency content beyond a step, so the Laplacian level is zero.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the 2D complex Gabor filter expression. Show that the Fourier transform of a 2D Gabor function is again a Gabor function. State three parameter choices used in industrial texture analysis. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the Mallat algorithm for 2D Discrete Wavelet Transform with a clear block diagram. Apply a single level of Daubechies-2 (D4) DWT on the row vector $x = [4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18]$ and report the approximation and detail coefficients. Show the Quadrature Mirror Filter relationship. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gabor filter derivation (7 marks)**

Step 1 (1 mark) — Begin with the 1D complex Gabor elementary function:
$$g(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \exp\!\left(i\omega_0 t\right)$$
This is the unique function that achieves the lower bound of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle $\Delta t \cdot \Delta\omega \geq 1/2$.

Step 2 (2 marks) — Extend to 2D by introducing rotation $\theta$ and aspect ratio $\gamma$:
$$g_{\lambda,\theta,\psi,\sigma,\gamma}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2 + \gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\exp\!\left(i\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}+\psi\right)\right)$$
where $x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ and $y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$.

Step 3 (2 marks) — Show that the Fourier transform of a 2D Gabor is again a 2D Gabor:
The Fourier transform of $g$ is concentrated around the carrier frequency $(\omega_{0}\cos\theta, \omega_{0}\sin\theta)$ with a Gaussian envelope of width inversely proportional to $\sigma$. The closed form is:
$$\mathcal{F}\{g\}(u,v) = C \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2(u'^2 + v'^2/\gamma^2)\right)\delta\!\left(u'-\tfrac{1}{\lambda}\right)$$
This duality means Gabor filters are *jointly optimal* in the space-frequency plane.

Step 4 (1 mark) — Real and imaginary parts are in quadrature:
$$g_{\text{even}}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2+\gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\cos\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}\right)$$
$$g_{\text{odd}}(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2+\gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\sin\!\left(2\pi\frac{x'}{\lambda}\right)$$

Step 5 (1 mark) — State three industrial parameter choices:
(i) $\lambda = 8$ pixels, $\sigma = 4$ pixels → spatial frequency $\approx 0.125$ cycles/pixel.
(ii) $\theta \in \{0°, 45°, 90°, 135°\}$ → 4-orientation filter bank.
(iii) $\gamma = 0.5$ → elliptical envelope, oriented stripes.

**(b) DWT Mallat algorithm and computation (7 marks)**

Step 1 (1 mark) — State the QMF relationship for perfect reconstruction:
$$g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$$
and the perfect-reconstruction condition $H(z)H(z^{-1}) + H(-z)H(-z^{-1}) = 2$.

Step 2 (2 marks) — Draw the Mallat tree (see Section 4.3): input → row-wise filters → downsample → column-wise filters → downsample → four sub-bands (LL, LH, HL, HH). State that for a 2D image the 1D DWT is applied first to rows, then to columns.

Step 3 (1 mark) — Daubechies-2 (D4) low-pass filter coefficients:
$$h \approx [0.4830,\; 0.8365,\; 0.2241,\; -0.1294]$$
High-pass from QMF: $g \approx [-0.1294,\; -0.2241,\; 0.8365,\; -0.4830]$.

Step 4 (2 marks) — Apply D4 to $x = [4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18]$ (periodic extension, downsample by 2):

**Approximation coefficients:**
$$cA_1[0] = 0.4830(4) + 0.8365(18) + 0.2241(17) + (-0.1294)(16) = 1.932 + 15.057 + 3.810 - 2.070 = 18.729$$
$$cA_1[1] = 0.4830(6) + 0.8365(5) + 0.2241(4) + (-0.1294)(3) = 2.898 + 4.183 + 0.896 - 0.388 = 7.589$$
$$cA_1[2] = 0.4830(8) + 0.8365(7) + 0.2241(6) + (-0.1294)(5) = 3.864 + 5.856 + 1.345 - 0.647 = 10.418$$
$$cA_1[3] = 0.4830(10) + 0.8365(9) + 0.2241(8) + (-0.1294)(7) = 4.830 + 7.529 + 1.793 - 0.906 = 13.246$$

**Detail coefficients:**
$$cD_1[0] = -0.1294(4) - 0.2241(18) + 0.8365(17) - 0.4830(16) = -0.518 - 4.034 + 14.221 - 7.728 = 1.941$$
$$cD_1[1] = -0.1294(6) - 0.2241(5) + 0.8365(4) - 0.4830(3) = -0.776 - 1.121 + 3.346 - 1.449 = 0.000$$
$$cD_1[2] = -0.1294(8) - 0.2241(7) + 0.8365(6) - 0.4830(5) = -1.035 - 1.569 + 5.019 - 2.415 = 0.000$$
$$cD_1[3] = -0.1294(10) - 0.2241(9) + 0.8365(8) - 0.4830(7) = -1.294 - 2.017 + 6.692 - 3.381 = 0.000$$

Step 5 (1 mark) — Result:
$$cA_1 \approx [18.73,\; 7.59,\; 10.42,\; 13.25],\qquad cD_1 \approx [1.94,\; 0,\; 0,\; 0]$$
The detail coefficients are nearly zero beyond the first sample because the input is a smooth linear ramp — the DWT correctly represents it as a low-frequency approximation with negligible high-frequency content.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners deduct marks — common pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi\sigma^2)$ normalisation constant** in the 2D Gaussian — losses of 0.5 to 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing the Laplacian pyramid with the Laplacian operator**: the Laplacian pyramid level is *the difference between a Gaussian pyramid level and an EXPANDed next-coarser level*, NOT the per-pixel Laplacian operator $\nabla^2$.
> 3. **DWT detail filter is NOT just $h$ time-reversed**: students forget the $(-1)^n$ alternating sign, violating the QMF condition and losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Gabor parameter confusion**: $\sigma$ in a Gabor filter refers to the *envelope* width, not the sinusoidal wavelength $\lambda$. Mixing them up is a classic 0.5-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the semi-group proof step**: in derivation questions, students who only state "Gaussian is unique because it is smooth" without invoking Cauchy's functional equation lose 3 of 7 marks.
> 6. **Gabor even/odd symmetry**: students forget to mention that the real part is the *even* filter and the imaginary part is the *odd* filter — both are used together in quadrature for robust feature detection.
> 7. **DWT on 2D images**: students apply the 1D DWT directly to a 2D image without first doing rows, then columns — automatic 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Scale-Space Analysis**

- **Scale-space kernel is uniquely Gaussian** because of linearity, shift-invariance, and the semi-group property (Cauchy's functional equation).
- The **heat-diffusion equation** $\partial_{\sigma}L=\nabla^{2}L$ is the scale-space PDE; $\sigma$ plays the role of "diffusion time".
- The **Gaussian pyramid** is a stack of low-pass-filtered, decimated images.
- The **Laplacian pyramid** is the band-pass stack: $L_l = G_l - \text{EXPAND}(G_{l+1})$.
- **Difference of Gaussians (DoG)** approximates the Laplacian of Gaussian: $G_{k\sigma} - G_{\sigma}$.
- The **Laplacian of Gaussian** has a zero-crossing at radius $r = \sigma\sqrt{2}$, defining the *natural scale* of a blob.
- **Gaussian derivative filters** are obtained by differentiating the Gaussian kernel: they are Canny-edge and SIFT keypoint detectors.
- **Lindeberg's normalized derivative**: $\sigma^{n}\vert L_{x^{n}y^{m}}\vert$ is the multi-scale blob strength.
- A **Gabor filter** is a complex sinusoid $\times$ Gaussian envelope; its Fourier transform is again a Gabor filter (uniquely space-frequency optimal).
- The five Gabor parameters: $\lambda, \theta, \psi, \sigma, \gamma$.
- The **uncertainty principle** $\Delta x \cdot \Delta\omega \geq 1/2$ is achieved (lower bound) by the Gabor function.
- The **DWT** uses a QMF bank with $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$.
- The **Mallat algorithm** applies the 1D DWT first row-wise then column-wise to a 2D image, producing LL, LH, HL, HH sub-bands.
- **Daubechies-2 (D4) low-pass coefficients** are $\frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}}[(1+\sqrt{3}),(3+\sqrt{3}),(3-\sqrt{3}),(1-\sqrt{3})]$.
- The **perfect-reconstruction condition** is $H(z)H(z^{-1}) + H(-z)H(-z^{-1}) = 2$.
- **JPEG 2000 uses CDF 9/7** wavelet (lossy) and **LeGall 5/3** wavelet (lossless).
- **Octave** = doubling of $\sigma$; **interval per octave** typically $s = 3$ (recommended by Lindeberg).
- For **SIFT**, the constant $k = 2^{1/s} = \sqrt{2}$ is the recommended scale-multiplication factor.
- The **Canny edge detector** is $\partial_{x}G * f(x,y)$ and $\partial_{y}G * f(x,y)$.
- The **sum of all Laplacian pyramid levels** (after appropriate expansion) reconstructs the original image exactly.
- The **HH sub-band** of a DWT captures diagonal edges, LH captures horizontal edges, HL captures vertical edges, LL is the coarse approximation.
- **Engineering applications:** SIFT (Lowe 2004), fingerprint minutiae extraction (Gabor), JPEG 2000 (DWT), medical image fusion (Laplacian pyramid + DWT), robotic SLAM (multi-scale feature maps).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Scale-Space Analysis: Image Pyramids, Gaussian Derivative Filters, Gabor Filters, and DWT**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Region Growing, Edge-Based Approaches to Segmentation, Graph-Cut, Mean-Shift, MRFs, Texture Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT602 - ROBOTIC VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Region Growing, Edge-Based Approaches to Segmentation, Graph-Cut, Mean-Shift, MRFs, Texture Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image Segmentation – The Foundation

**Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (sets of pixels) such that pixels belonging to the same region share similar visual characteristics (intensity, color, texture) and are spatially connected, while pixels from different regions differ significantly in these properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Segmentation is the *bridge* between low-level image processing (pixels) and high-level scene understanding (objects). Every robotic vision pipeline uses segmentation as a critical pre-processing stage for tasks like object recognition, path planning, and visual servoing.

---

## 1.2 Region Growing

### Formal Definition
**Region Growing** is a pixel-based image segmentation technique that examines neighboring pixels of initial *seed points* and determines whether the pixel neighbors should be added to the region based on a predefined **similarity criterion** (homogeneity predicate), such as intensity range, color similarity, or texture properties.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **puddles of water forming on a flat surface** after rain. Each raindrop landing on the surface becomes a *seed point*. As more water accumulates, the puddle expands to include only those drops that "fit" (similar height/level) — it never crosses the physical boundaries (edges) of the surface. Similarly, in region growing, the algorithm starts from seed pixels and absorbs neighbors whose pixel values lie within a permitted tolerance window, stopping when the boundary criterion is violated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Components of Region Growing:**
> - **Seed Point Selection** – initial pixel(s) chosen either manually or via clustering
> - **Similarity Criterion** – homogeneity predicate (e.g., $\vert I(x,y) - \mu_R \vert < T$)
> - **Stopping Rule** – boundary condition (gradient threshold, region size limit)

---

## 1.3 Edge-Based Approaches to Segmentation

### Formal Definition
**Edge-Based Segmentation** is a class of image segmentation techniques that identify object boundaries by detecting **discontinuities in image intensity** (luminance, color, or texture) and then connecting these edge pixels to form closed contours that delineate homogeneous regions.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine tracing the outline of countries on a world map. The boundaries (edges) are where the *color, texture, or shading suddenly changes* from one region to another. Edge-based segmentation works exactly the same way — it locates the "ink lines" of the digital image and treats those lines as the borders between objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Edge Operators (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Gradient-based:** Sobel, Prewitt, Roberts
> - **Second-order:** Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG), Canny
> - **Canny Edge Detector** is widely accepted as *optimal* for robotic vision due to its three criteria: good detection, good localization, single response.

---

## 1.4 Graph-Cut Segmentation

### Formal Definition
**Graph-Cut** is an energy-minimization-based segmentation technique that models an image as a graph $G = (V, E)$ where pixels are *nodes* ($V$) and weighted edges ($E$) encode both **data fidelity** (unary/regional terms) and **boundary smoothness** (binary/Ising terms). The **minimum cut** (or *s*-*t* min-cut) of this graph yields the optimal binary or multi-label segmentation that globally minimizes the energy function.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a network of water pipes connecting two giant reservoirs (the *source* representing "object" and the *sink* representing "background"). Each pipe has a *capacity* (cost). The **min-cut** finds the cheapest set of pipes to sever so the source and sink are disconnected — that severing IS the segmentation boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boykov-Kolmogorov Algorithm** is the standard max-flow/min-cut solver used in KTU-level implementations. The energy function is **submodular** and **guaranteed to converge** to a local minimum near the global minimum.

---

## 1.5 Mean-Shift Segmentation

### Formal Definition
**Mean-Shift** is a non-parametric, iterative **mode-seeking clustering algorithm** that locates the densest regions (modes) of a feature-space distribution by shifting a sliding window toward the local mean of the data, with the kernel-weighted average. In image segmentation, it is applied in the joint spatial-range (spatial coordinates + color/intensity) domain.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a hill covered in fog. You want to find the peaks of hills. Starting at any point, you walk *uphill* in the direction where the slope is steepest (towards denser points), and you keep walking until you reach a flat region (a *mode* — local maximum). Multiple people starting at different positions will converge to different hilltops — each hilltop represents a distinct segment.

---

## 1.6 Markov Random Fields (MRFs) for Segmentation

### Formal Definition
A **Markov Random Field (MRF)** is an undirected probabilistic graphical model in which the joint probability of a label assignment $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ over image pixels is governed by **local Markovian dependencies** — meaning the label of any pixel depends only on the labels of its immediate neighborhood, and global joint distributions factorize as products of local *clique potentials*.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a chessboard where each square's color depends strongly on its immediate neighbors (you would never expect a black square in a white area unless constrained by alternating pattern). The global pattern is described by *local* interactions — this is the essence of MRFs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hammersley-Clifford Theorem** establishes the equivalence between MRFs and **Gibbs Random Fields (GRFs)** — the joint probability is a Gibbs distribution:
>
> $$P(X) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\frac{U(X)}{T}\right)$$
>
> where $U(X)$ is the *energy function* and $Z$ is the partition function.

---

## 1.7 Texture Segmentation

### Formal Definition
**Texture Segmentation** is the process of partitioning an image into regions that are homogeneous in their **textural properties** — spatial patterns characterized by repeating local structures with specific orientation, scale, periodicity, and randomness (e.g., grass, brick walls, fabric, wood grain).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine walking through a tile-floor corridor that transitions into a carpeted room. The floor pattern itself *tells* you which area is which. Texture segmentation algorithms work like a robotic "tactile" perception of the image — they "feel" the local surface structure of pixel neighborhoods and group similar-feeling patches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Texture Feature Extractors (KTU Syllabus):**
> - **Statistical:** Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), Local Binary Patterns (LBP)
> - **Filter-based:** Gabor filters, Laws' texture energy
> - **Structural:** Autocorrelation, Fractal dimension
> - **Model-based:** Markov Random Fields, Wavelet transforms

---

## 1.8 Why This Topic Matters in Robotic Vision

In **KTU 2024 scheme assessments**, segmentation forms a critical bridge for autonomous robotics:
- **Industrial bin-picking:** separating touching objects
- **Autonomous navigation:** road vs. off-road detection
- **Medical robotics:** tumor boundary delineation
- **Human-robot interaction:** gesture and face segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Region Growing – Algorithm Logic

**Step 1:** Choose seed pixels $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ either:
- Manually (user-selected)
- Automatically (lowest intensity, highest gradient, or from clustering)

**Step 2:** For each seed $s_i$, initialize region $R_i$ with $R_i \leftarrow \{s_i\}$. Compute region property $\mu_i$ (e.g., mean intensity).

**Step 3:** Examine all unallocated 4-connected (or 8-connected) neighbors $N(x,y)$ of pixels in $R_i$.

**Step 4:** Apply homogeneity predicate:
- Pixel $p$ joins $R_i$ **iff** $\vert I(p) - \mu_i \vert \leq T$ (intensity-based)

**Step 5:** Update $\mu_i \leftarrow \dfrac{1}{\vert R_i \vert}\sum_{p \in R_i} I(p)$ after each addition.

**Step 6:** Repeat Steps 3–5 until no more pixels satisfy the criterion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Region Growing?**
> - Connects pixels that are *both* similar *and* spatially adjacent — produces **closed, connected regions** by design.
> - The "Why": Overcomes the noise sensitivity of pure thresholding while preserving region coherence.
> - **The "How"**: The greedy acceptance rule ensures monotonic growth; convergence is guaranteed in finite steps.

### Critical Limitations
- Sensitive to **seed selection**
- **Order of processing** may affect final result
- Performs poorly on **noisy images** without pre-smoothing

---

## 2.2 Edge-Based Approaches – Detailed Mechanics

### Gradient-Based Detection
The image gradient $\nabla I = (G_x, G_y)$ is computed via convolution:
$$G_x = I \ast K_x, \quad G_y = I \ast K_y$$
where $K_x, K_y$ are derivative kernels (Sobel, Prewitt).

**Gradient magnitude:**
$$M(x,y) = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2} = \vert \nabla I(x,y) \vert$$

**Gradient direction:**
$$\theta(x,y) = \arctan\left(\frac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$$

### Canny Edge Detection – The Gold Standard
1. **Gaussian smoothing:** $I_g = G_\sigma \ast I$
2. **Gradient computation** using Sobel
3. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** thins edges to single-pixel width
4. **Double thresholding** with $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$
5. **Edge tracking by hysteresis:** strong edges + connected weak edges

### Linking Edge Pixels into Contours
Two classical methods:
- **Hysteresis thresholding** (Canny)
- **Boundary following** (active contours / snakes)

---

## 2.3 Graph-Cut – Deep Energy Formulation

### The Segmentation Energy
For a binary segmentation with labels $L \in \{0, 1\}$ (background/object) and observed image data $I$:
$$E(L) = \underbrace{\sum_{p \in V} D_p(L_p)}_{\text{Data Term (Regional)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{(p,q) \in N} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)}_{\text{Smoothness Term (Boundary)}}$$

**Where:**
- $D_p(L_p) = -\ln P(I_p \vert L_p)$ – unary cost (likelihood)
- $V_{pq}(L_p, L_q) = \lambda \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{(I_p - I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot [L_p \neq L_q]$ – Potts model pairwise cost
- $\lambda$ – regularization weight
- $\sigma$ – noise scale

### The Min-Cut Theorem (Ford-Fulkerson)
**Theorem:** For a graph $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$ and sink $t$, the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ equals the capacity of the minimum $s$–$t$ cut.

$$\max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{C} \text{cap}(C)$$

The min-cut directly partitions the graph into source-side (object) and sink-side (background) regions, **minimizing** the segmentation energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Graph-cut is used in:
> - Medical image segmentation (tumor, organ delineation)
> - Foreground extraction in photo editors (e.g., "Background Eraser")
> - Stereo correspondence and multi-view reconstruction
> - Interactive segmentation with brush strokes (lazy snapping)

---

## 2.4 Mean-Shift – The Iterative Mode-Seeking Procedure

### Mathematical Foundation
For $n$ data points $x_i$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and a kernel $K(x)$ with bandwidth $h$:
$$m(x) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)} - x$$

where $g(x) = -K'(x)$ is the **shadow** (negative derivative) of the kernel profile $K(x)$.

### Common Kernel Choices
- **Gaussian:** $K(x) = \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right)$, $g(x) = \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right)$
- **Epanechnikov:** $K(x) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - x^2)$ for $\vert x \vert \leq 1$

### Mean-Shift Algorithm Steps
1. Initialize window center $y_k = x$
2. Compute mean-shift vector $m(y_k)$
3. Update: $y_{k+1} = y_k + m(y_k)$
4. Repeat until $\vert y_{k+1} - y_k \vert < \epsilon$

### Segmentation via Mean-Shift
Apply mean-shift in the **joint spatial-range domain** $\mathbf{x} = (x_s, y_s, r, g, b)$:
- **Spatial bandwidth** $h_s$: controls region size
- **Range bandwidth** $h_r$: controls color/intensity similarity

After convergence, all pixels that converge to the same mode are merged into one segment.

---

## 2.5 MRFs – Mathematical Theory

### Markov Property
$$P(x_i \vert X_{V \setminus \{i\}}) = P(x_i \vert N_i)$$

The label at pixel $i$ depends only on the labels in its neighborhood $N_i$.

### Joint Distribution (Gibbs Equivalence)
$$P(X) = \frac{1}{Z} \prod_{c \in C} \psi_c(X_c) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\sum_{c \in C} V_c(X_c)\right)$$

where:
- $C$ – set of all cliques in the neighborhood system
- $\psi_c$ – clique potential function
- $V_c$ – clique energy (cost)
- $Z = \sum_X \exp\left(-\sum_{c} V_c(X_c)\right)$ – partition function

### Optimization Techniques
- **Iterated Conditional Modes (ICM)** – greedy, fast, locally optimal
- **Simulated Annealing** – slow, globally optimal in the limit
- **Graph Cuts** – exact for submodular energies
- **Belief Propagation** – message passing, approximate

### MAP-MRF Segmentation
The Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimate:
$$X^* = \arg\max_X P(X \vert I) = \arg\min_X \left[ \sum_{p} -\ln P(I_p \vert X_p) + \sum_{(p,q)} \beta \cdot [X_p \neq X_q] \right]$$

This MAP-MRF energy is **identical in form** to the Graph-Cut energy — establishing a deep theoretical link between the two methods.

---

## 2.6 Texture Segmentation – Feature Extraction Theory

### Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)
For an image with $N_g$ gray levels, the GLCM $C(i, j, d, \theta)$ counts occurrences of gray-level $i$ adjacent to gray-level $j$ at offset $(d, \theta)$.

**Haralick Texture Features (KTU High-Yield):**

$$\text{Energy} = \sum_{i,j} C(i,j)^2$$

$$\text{Contrast} = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 C(i,j)$$

$$\text{Homogeneity} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{C(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2}$$

$$\text{Correlation} = \frac{\sum_{i,j}(i-\mu_i)(j-\mu_j)C(i,j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$$

$$\text{Entropy} = -\sum_{i,j} C(i,j) \log C(i,j)$$

### Gabor Filter Bank
A 2D Gabor filter is a Gaussian-modulated complex sinusoid:
$$G(x, y; \lambda, \theta, \psi, \sigma, \gamma) = \exp\left(-\frac{x'^2 + \gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cos\left(2\pi \frac{x'}{\lambda} + \psi\right)$$

where $x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ and $y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$.

By varying $\lambda$ (scale) and $\theta$ (orientation), a multi-resolution filter bank captures texture at multiple spatial frequencies and orientations.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Core Formula | Purpose |
|---|--------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | Region Growing | $\vert I(p) - \mu_R \vert < T$ | Homogeneity predicate |
| 2 | Gradient Magnitude | $M = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Edge strength |
| 3 | Canny Edge | $M$ + NMS + Hysteresis | Optimal edges |
| 4 | Graph-Cut Energy | $E(L) = \sum D_p + \sum V_{pq}$ | Minimize globally |
| 5 | Max-Flow Min-Cut | $\max \vert f \vert = \min \text{cap}(C)$ | Optimal partition |
| 6 | Mean-Shift Vector | $m(x) = \frac{\sum x_i g(\cdot)}{\sum g(\cdot)} - x$ | Mode seeking |
| 7 | MRF Probability | $P(X) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp(-U(X)/T)$ | Joint distribution |
| 8 | MAP-MRF Estimate | $X^* = \arg\min E_{\text{data}} + E_{\text{smooth}}$ | Optimal labels |
| 9 | GLCM Energy | $\sum_{i,j} C(i,j)^2$ | Texture uniformity |
| 10 | GLCM Contrast | $\sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 C(i,j)$ | Local intensity variation |
| 11 | GLCM Entropy | $-\sum_{i,j} C \log C$ | Texture randomness |
| 12 | 2D Gabor | $\exp(\cdot)\cos(2\pi x'/\lambda + \psi)$ | Multi-scale texture |
| 13 | Potts Model | $\lambda \cdot [L_p \neq L_q]$ | Boundary cost |
| 14 | Region Mean Update | $\mu_{n+1} = \mu_n + \frac{(I - \mu_n)}{n+1}$ | Incremental update |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean-Shift Update Equation

The goal is to derive the iterative mean-shift formula starting from the kernel density estimate.

### Step 1: Kernel Density Estimate (KDE)
Given $n$ data points $x_i$, the kernel density estimate at $x$ is:
$$\hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)$$

where $K(\cdot)$ is a radially symmetric kernel and $h$ is the bandwidth.

### Step 2: Compute the Gradient
To find the mode (maximum density), take the gradient with respect to $x$:
$$\nabla \hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \nabla K\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)$$

### Step 3: Use Kernel Profile Notation
For a radially symmetric kernel, $K(x) = k(\Vert x \Vert^2)$. The gradient is:
$$\nabla K(x) = 2k'(\Vert x \Vert^2) \cdot x$$

Define $g(x) = -k'(x)$ (the shadow/profile of the kernel). Then:
$$\nabla K(x) = -2 g(\Vert x \Vert^2) \cdot x$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the Gradient
$$\nabla \hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[-2 g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right) \cdot \left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{h}\right]$$

$$\nabla \hat{f}(x) = \frac{-2}{n h^{d+2}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x - x_i) \cdot g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)$$

### Step 5: Set the Gradient to Zero (Mode Condition)
At a mode, $\nabla \hat{f}(x) = 0$. The kernel factor can be moved to the denominator:
$$\frac{1}{2} \nabla \hat{f}(x) \propto \left[ \underbrace{\frac{\sum_{i} x_i \, g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}{\sum_{i} g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}}_{\text{Weighted Mean}} - x \right] \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{n h^{d+2}} \sum_i g(\cdot)}_{\text{Positive Scalar}} = 0$$

### Step 6: Define the Mean-Shift Vector
The term in brackets must be zero, defining the **mean-shift vector**:
$$m(x) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)} - x$$

### Step 7: Iterative Update Rule
The mode is reached by following this vector:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + m(x_k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x_k - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x_k - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}$$

This convergent update **always climbs the density gradient** — a beautiful property with no learning rate to tune.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Graph-Cut Energy from MAP-MRF Framework

### Step 1: Posterior Probability
By Bayes' theorem, the posterior over label field $X$ given observed image $I$:
$$P(X \vert I) = \frac{P(I \vert X) \cdot P(X)}{P(I)}$$

Since $P(I)$ is constant (data is observed), maximizing the posterior is equivalent to:
$$X^* = \arg\max_X \left[ \log P(I \vert X) + \log P(X) \right]$$

### Step 2: Likelihood (Data Term) — Assume Pixel Independence
$$P(I \vert X) = \prod_{p \in V} P(I_p \vert X_p)$$

### Step 3: Prior (Smoothness Term) — Gibbs Distribution
$$P(X) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\sum_{c \in C} V_c(X_c)\right)$$

### Step 4: Combine the Two Terms
$$\log P(I \vert X) + \log P(X) = \sum_{p} \log P(I_p \vert X_p) - \sum_{c} V_c(X_c) - \log Z$$

Drop the constant $\log Z$ and multiply by $-1$ to convert to a minimization:
$$X^* = \arg\min_X \left[ -\sum_p \log P(I_p \vert X_p) + \sum_c V_c(X_c) \right]$$

### Step 5: Define Energy Functional
$$E(X) = E_{\text{data}}(X) + E_{\text{smooth}}(X)$$

with:
- $E_{\text{data}}(X) = \sum_p D_p(X_p) = -\sum_p \log P(I_p \vert X_p)$
- $E_{\text{smooth}}(X) = \sum_{(p,q) \in N} V_{pq}(X_p, X_q)$ (pairwise clique restriction)

### Step 6: Potts Model Parameterization
$$V_{pq}(X_p, X_q) = \lambda \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(I_p - I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot [X_p \neq X_q]$$

where:
- $\lambda > 0$ balances data fidelity vs. smoothness
- $\sigma$ sets the edge-sensitivity scale
- $[X_p \neq X_q]$ is the Iverson bracket (1 if different, 0 otherwise)

### Step 7: Min-Cut Solves MAP-MRF
For a **submodular** energy (where $V_{pq}(0,0) + V_{pq}(1,1) \leq V_{pq}(0,1) + V_{pq}(1,0)$), the global minimum of $E(X)$ is computed exactly by a **single $s$–$t$ min-cut** (Greig et al., 1989).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Bridge:** The Potts-model energy is submodular and corresponds to a graph where terminal-edge weights encode $D_p$ and neighbor-edge weights encode $V_{pq}$. Min-cut then solves the NP-hard labeling problem in polynomial time.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example – Region Growing

**Image:** $3 \times 3$ grayscale patch with values:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 50 & 52 & 100 \\ 48 & 51 & 105 \\ 49 & 53 & 102 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Seed:** center pixel at $(2,2)$ with intensity $I(2,2) = 51$.
**Threshold:** $T = 4$
**Connectivity:** 4-connected

### Step 1: Initialize Region
$R = \{(2,2)\}$, $\mu_R = 51$

### Step 2: Examine Neighbors of $(2,2)$
Neighbors: $(1,2) = 52$, $(3,2) = 53$, $(2,1) = 48$, $(2,3) = 105$

| Pixel | Intensity | $\vert I - \mu_R \vert$ | $\leq T$? | Action |
|-------|-----------|------------------------|-----------|--------|
| (1,2) | 52 | 1 | Yes | Add to $R$ |
| (3,2) | 53 | 2 | Yes | Add to $R$ |
| (2,1) | 48 | 3 | Yes | Add to $R$ |
| (2,3) | 105 | 54 | No | Reject |

### Step 3: Update Region Mean
After adding 3 pixels:
$$\mu_R = \frac{52 + 53 + 48 + 51}{4} = \frac{204}{4} = 51.0$$

### Step 4: Examine New Neighbors
Now examine neighbors of newly added pixels (excluding already in $R$):
- $(1,1) = 50$: $\vert 50 - 51 \vert = 1 \leq 4$ ✓
- $(1,3) = 100$: $\vert 100 - 51 \vert = 49 > 4$ ✗
- $(3,1) = 49$: $\vert 49 - 51 \vert = 2 \leq 4$ ✓
- $(3,3) = 102$: $\vert 102 - 51 \vert = 51 > 4$ ✗

### Step 5: Final Region
$R = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2), (2,3) \text{ rejected}, (3,1), (3,2)\}$
$R = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2), (3,1), (3,2)\}$ — 6 pixels in homogeneous region

### Step 6: Update Final Mean
$$\mu_R = \frac{50 + 52 + 48 + 51 + 49 + 53}{6} = \frac{303}{6} = 50.5$$

The region terminates at the **bright pixel cluster** (intensities 100, 102, 105) which is correctly identified as a distinct segment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Region Growing | Edge-Based | Graph-Cut | Mean-Shift | MRF | Texture |
|-----------|----------------|------------|-----------|------------|-----|---------|
| **Principle** | Pixel similarity | Discontinuity | Energy min | Mode seeking | Probabilistic | Feature clustering |
| **Global Optimality** | No (greedy) | No (local) | Yes (min-cut) | No (local modes) | MAP ≈ Yes (for submodular) | No (depends) |
| **Closed Contours** | Yes | Needs post-link | Yes | No (merges needed) | Yes | Yes |
| **Noise Robustness** | Low | Low | High | High | High | Medium |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | High (polynomial) | Medium | High | High |
| **Number of Parameters** | 1 (T) | 2 (low, high) | 2 (λ, σ) | 2 (h_s, h_r) | 2 (β, T) | Many (filter bank) |
| **Best Use Case** | Small images, medical | Real-time | High-quality | Color segmentation | Noisy, complex | Natural scenes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Region Growing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Seed Selection]
    B --> C[Initialize Region R with Seeds]
    C --> D[Compute Region Property mu R]
    D --> E[Examine 4 or 8 Neighbors of R]
    E --> F{Similarity Test I p minus mu R leq T}
    F -- Yes --> G[Add Pixel p to R]
    G --> H[Update mu R]
    H --> I{More Unvisited Neighbors}
    I -- Yes --> E
    I -- No --> J[Final Region R Output]
    F -- No --> K[Reject Pixel p]
    K --> I
    J --> L[Post Processing Merge Small Regions]
    L --> M[Segmented Output]
```

## 4.2 Graph-Cut Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodes [Image as Graph]
        P1[Pixel p1]
        P2[Pixel p2]
        P3[Pixel p3]
        P4[Pixel p4]
    end
    S[Source S Object] -.->|Unary Dp obj| P1
    S -.->|Unary Dp obj| P2
    T[Sink T Background] -.->|Unary Dp bkg| P3
    T -.->|Unary Dp bkg| P4
    P1 ---|Pairwise V12| P2
    P2 ---|Pairwise V23| P3
    P3 ---|Pairwise V34| P4
    P1 ---|Pairwise V14| P4
    C[Min Cut Algorithm] --> Result[Object vs Background Labels]
    S --> C
    T --> C
    P1 --> C
    P2 --> C
    P3 --> C
    P4 --> C
```

## 4.3 Mean-Shift Convergence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initialize Window at x0] --> Eval[Compute Kernel Weighted Mean]
    Eval --> Calc[Calculate Mean Shift Vector m x]
    Calc --> Move[Update Position x1 = x0 + m x]
    Move --> Check{Convergence Norm m x leq epsilon}
    Check -- No --> Eval
    Check -- Yes --> Converge[Converged Mode Reached]
    Converge --> Group[Merge Pixels Sharing Same Mode]
    Group --> Output[Segmented Image]
```

## 4.4 MRF Segmentation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[Observed Image I] --> FE[Feature Extraction Pixel Intensities]
    FE --> PD[Prior Model P X Gibbs]
    PD --> CL[Clique Potentials Vc]
    FE --> LH[Likelihood P I given X]
    LH --> DT[Data Energy Ed]
    CL --> ST[Smoothness Energy Es]
    DT --> Comb[Combine E = Ed + Es]
    ST --> Comb
    Comb --> Opt[Optimization ICM or Graph Cut or BP]
    Opt --> Map[MAP Estimate X star]
    Map --> Seg[Final Segmentation]
```

## 4.5 Texture Segmentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Image] --> Pre[Preprocessing Gaussian Smooth]
    Pre --> GB[Gabor Filter Bank Multi Scale Multi Orientation]
    GB --> Feat[Feature Vector per Pixel]
    Feat --> Clust[Clustering K means or Mean Shift]
    Clust --> Post[Post processing Region Refinement]
    Post --> Out[Texture Segmented Output]
    Input -.->|Statistical| GLCM[GLCM Computation]
    GLCM --> Har[Haralick Features]
    Har --> Clust
```

## 4.6 Comparative Method Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Image Characteristics] --> Q1{Need Closed Contours}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Image Noisy}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Mean Shift]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Graph Cut or MRF]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Region Growing or Graph Cut]
    Q --> Q6{Texture Important}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use Gabor or GLCM + Clustering]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Use Edge or Region Based]
    Q --> Q9{Real Time Constraint}
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[Use Edge Based Sobel or Canny]
    Q9 -- No --> Q11[Use Graph Cut or MRF for Optimality]
```
