<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain the concept of **region growing** in image segmentation. List any **two** seed selection strategies.

**Model Answer:**
Region growing is a pixel-based segmentation technique that groups pixels with similar properties (intensity, color, texture) starting from initial seed points. The algorithm examines 4-connected or 8-connected neighbors of the seed and adds those satisfying a homogeneity criterion (e.g., $\vert I(p) - \mu_R \vert \leq T$) to the region. The process continues iteratively until no more pixels meet the criterion.

**Two seed selection strategies:**
1. **Manual selection** – user clicks on representative points of each object.
2. **Automatic selection** – seeds chosen as local intensity minima/maxima, or via K-means clustering of intensity histograms.
3. **Grid-based uniform seeding** – seeds placed at fixed intervals across the image.

*(Any two valid strategies accepted – 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each strategy, 1 mark for criterion.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **edge-based segmentation** and **region-based segmentation**. State one limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Edge-Based | Region-Based |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Principle** | Detects intensity discontinuities (boundaries) | Groups pixels with similar properties |
| **Output** | Edge pixels / contours | Connected homogeneous regions |
| **Approach** | Top-down (find boundaries) | Bottom-up (grow/shrink regions) |
| **Example** | Canny, Sobel | Region growing, split-and-merge |

**Limitations:**
- **Edge-based limitation:** Produces broken / disconnected edges; post-processing (linking) is required to form closed contours. Sensitive to noise.
- **Region-based limitation:** Sensitive to seed point selection and order of processing; computationally expensive for large images.

---

## 5.2 PART B – 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) – Graph-Cut and Mean-Shift Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** 

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Graph-Cut** formulation for image segmentation. Define the energy function and explain each term. State and prove the **Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Mean-Shift vector** update equation. Explain how Mean-Shift is used for color image segmentation in the joint spatial-range domain. Discuss its advantages over K-means clustering. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Graph-Cut Formulation:**

Model the image as a graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of pixels + two terminal nodes (source $S$ = "object", sink $T$ = "background")
- $E$ = set of edges (n-links between pixels + t-links to terminals)

**Energy Function:**
$$E(L) = \sum_{p \in V} D_p(L_p) + \sum_{(p,q) \in N} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$$

| Term | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|------|---------|---------------------------|
| $D_p(L_p)$ | Data cost: $-\ln P(I_p \vert L_p)$ | How well does pixel fit the "object" or "background" model? |
| $V_{pq}$ | Smoothness cost: Potts model penalty | How likely are neighboring pixels to share the same label? |

**Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:**

*Statement:* The maximum value of a flow from source $S$ to sink $T$ in a network equals the minimum capacity of all $S$–$T$ cuts.

*Proof Sketch:*
1. Let $f^*$ be a maximum flow. Construct cut $C^*$ by setting:
   - $S$-side = all vertices reachable from $S$ in residual graph $G_f^*$
   - $T$-side = remaining vertices
2. No edge crosses $C^*$ with unused capacity (else $f^*$ wouldn't be maximum).
3. Hence $\text{cap}(C^*) = \vert f^* \vert$.
4. For any other cut $C$, all flow must cross $C$, so $\vert f^* \vert \leq \text{cap}(C)$.
5. Therefore $\vert f^* \vert = \min_C \text{cap}(C)$. $\blacksquare$

**Application:** The min-cut partitions pixels into $S$-side (labeled "object") and $T$-side (labeled "background"), **globally minimizing** $E(L)$ when the energy is submodular.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Derivation of Mean-Shift Vector:**

Starting from kernel density estimate (full derivation in Section 3.1):
$$\hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{n h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)$$

Setting $\nabla \hat{f}(x) = 0$ with $K(x) = k(\Vert x \Vert^2)$ and $g(x) = -k'(x)$:

$$m(x) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)} - x$$

**Iterative update:** $x_{k+1} = x_k + m(x_k)$

**Application to Color Image Segmentation:**

1. Represent each pixel as a 5-D point: $\mathbf{x} = (x_s, y_s, L, a, b)$ in CIELAB space
2. Choose two bandwidths: $h_s$ (spatial) and $h_r$ (range/color)
3. For each pixel, run mean-shift iteration until convergence
4. **Cluster:** Group all pixels that converged to the same mode (within $h_s$ and $h_r$)
5. Assign each pixel the color of its converged mode → segmented image

**Advantages over K-means:**
- **No fixed cluster count** required (K-means needs $k$)
- **Adaptive cluster shape** (K-means assumes spherical)
- **Robust to outliers** (kernel weighting reduces noise influence)
- **Convergence guaranteed** to local modes (K-means may diverge in degenerate cases)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Graph-Cut/Mean-Shift:**
> Students often forget to (1) state that the energy is **submodular** for min-cut to apply, (2) mention **both** bandwidths $h_s$ and $h_r$ for mean-shift, and (3) confuse the **kernel profile** $k(x)$ with the **shadow function** $g(x)$. Deductions of 1–2 marks per omission are common.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – MRF and Texture Segmentation Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **Markov Random Fields (MRF)** for image segmentation. State the Hammersley-Clifford theorem and derive the MAP-MRF energy. Compare MRF with Graph-Cut. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **Texture Segmentation**? Explain the **Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)** and derive any **four Haralick texture features** with their physical significance. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Markov Random Fields:**

An MRF is a probabilistic model where the label of a pixel depends only on the labels of its neighbors:
$$P(x_i \vert X_{V \setminus \{i\}}) = P(x_i \vert N_i)$$

**Hammersley-Clifford Theorem:**

*Statement:* A random field $X$ is an MRF with respect to neighborhood system $N$ **if and only if** its joint distribution is a **Gibbs Random Field**:
$$P(X) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{T} \sum_{c \in C} V_c(X_c)\right)$$

**Derivation of MAP-MRF Energy:**

By Bayes' rule (as in Section 3.2):
$$X^* = \arg\max_X P(X \vert I) = \arg\max_X P(I \vert X) P(X)$$

Taking log and applying the Gibbs form:
$$X^* = \arg\min_X \left[ \underbrace{-\sum_p \log P(I_p \vert X_p)}_{E_{\text{data}}} + \underbrace{\sum_{(p,q)} V_{pq}(X_p, X_q)}_{E_{\text{smooth}}} \right]$$

**Comparison: MRF vs Graph-Cut**

| Aspect | MRF | Graph-Cut |
|--------|-----|-----------|
| **Framework** | Probabilistic | Combinatorial |
| **Objective** | MAP estimate | Energy minimization |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal (submodular) | Globally optimal (submodular) |
| **Solution Methods** | ICM, Simulated Annealing, BP | Min-cut (Boykov-Kolmogorov) |
| **Energy Form** | Gibbs form | Sum of unary + pairwise |
| **Theoretical Link** | MAP-MRF ⟺ Min-Cut (submodular case) | Direct min-cut |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Texture Segmentation:**

Texture segmentation partitions an image into regions that are homogeneous in their textural properties. Unlike intensity segmentation, it considers local spatial patterns (variation, directionality, coarseness).

**Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM):**

The GLCM $C(i,j,d,\theta)$ counts how often a pixel with intensity $i$ has a neighbor of intensity $j$ at displacement $(d, \theta)$:
$$C(i,j,d,\theta) = \#\{(p,q) : I(p) = i, I(p + \Delta) = j, \vert \Delta \vert = d, \angle \Delta = \theta\}$$

The matrix is then **normalized** by dividing by total count.

**Four Haralick Features:**

**1. Energy (Angular Second Moment):**
$$E = \sum_{i=0}^{N_g-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N_g-1} C(i,j)^2$$
*Physical meaning:* High energy indicates a homogeneous, smooth texture; low energy indicates a complex, varied texture.

**2. Contrast:**
$$C = \sum_{i=0}^{N_g-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N_g-1} (i-j)^2 \cdot C(i,j)$$
*Physical meaning:* Measures the local intensity variation. High contrast = strong edges/grain.

**3. Homogeneity (Inverse Difference Moment):**
$$H = \sum_{i=0}^{N_g-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N_g-1} \frac{C(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2}$$
*Physical meaning:* High homogeneity = smooth, slowly varying texture.

**4. Entropy:**
$$S = -\sum_{i=0}^{N_g-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N_g-1} C(i,j) \log C(i,j)$$
*Physical meaning:* High entropy = random, disordered texture (e.g., noise, sand).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – MRF & Texture:**
> Common errors: (1) Students fail to **normalize** the GLCM before applying formulas, (2) forget to **define displacement** $(d, \theta)$ explicitly, (3) do not state the **physical interpretation** of Haralick features (deducted 1 mark each), (4) confuse **MRF and GRF** as different models (they are equivalent by Hammersley-Clifford).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Region Growing:** Seed + similarity + stopping rule. Sensitive to noise and seed choice.
- **Edge-Based:** Canny = Gaussian → Gradient → NMS → Hysteresis. Edges must be linked.
- **Graph-Cut:** $E(L) = \sum D_p + \sum V_{pq}$. Solved by min-cut (submodular). Globally optimal.
- **Max-Flow Min-Cut:** $\max |f| = \min \text{cap}(C)$. Ford-Fulkerson theorem.
- **Mean-Shift:** Mode-seeking in joint spatial-range domain. $h_s$ and $h_r$ control output.
- **MRF:** Markov property = local dependence. Equivalent to Gibbs by H-C theorem.
- **MAP-MRF:** $X^* = \arg\min E_{\text{data}} + E_{\text{smooth}}$.
- **Texture:** GLCM, Haralick features, Gabor filter banks.
- **Hammersley-Clifford:** MRF ⟺ GRF (Gibbs distribution).
- **Submodularity:** Required condition for min-cut to solve MAP-MRF exactly.

### High-Yield Formulas (Must Remember)
- Region predicate: $\vert I(p) - \mu_R \vert \leq T$
- Gradient magnitude: $M = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$
- Graph-Cut energy: $E(L) = \sum D_p + \sum V_{pq}$
- Potts model: $V_{pq} = \lambda \exp\left(-\frac{(I_p-I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) [L_p \neq L_q]$
- Mean-shift: $m(x) = \frac{\sum x_i g(\Vert \frac{x-x_i}{h}\Vert^2)}{\sum g(\Vert \frac{x-x_i}{h}\Vert^2)} - x$
- MRF joint: $P(X) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\left(-\frac{1}{T}\sum_c V_c(X_c)\right)$
- GLCM Contrast: $\sum (i-j)^2 C(i,j)$
- GLCM Energy: $\sum C(i,j)^2$
- GLCM Entropy: $-\sum C \log C$
- 2D Gabor: Gaussian × Complex sinusoid

### Quick Selection Heuristic for Exam
| If the question says... | Use this method... |
|------------------------|--------------------|
| "homogeneous regions" | Region Growing |
| "boundaries" / "contours" | Edge-Based (Canny) |
| "global optimum" / "min-cut" | Graph-Cut |
| "no prior cluster count" | Mean-Shift |
| "noisy image" / "Bayesian" | MRF |
| "natural textures" / "GLCM" | Texture (Haralick) |

### Frequently Asked Cross-Connections (High-Scoring Points)
1. MAP-MRF energy and Graph-Cut energy are **mathematically equivalent** for submodular pairwise terms — establish the link for bonus marks.
2. Mean-Shift with Gaussian kernel is the **same as kernel density gradient ascent**.
3. Canny's hysteresis is essentially a **two-threshold connectivity rule** — similar in spirit to region growing.
4. Gabor filters are **bandpass filters** whose frequency response matches mammalian V1 cortex.

### Last-Minute Mnemonics
- **"S³R"** for Region Growing: **S**eeds, **S**imilarity, **S**topping **R**ule
- **"DSV"** for Graph-Cut: **D**ata + **S**moothness = **V** (Energy)
- **"MAGG"** for Mean-Shift: **M**ode-seeking, **A**daptive, **G**radient-free, **G**aussian-friendly
- **"HCE"** for MRF: **H**ammersley-**C**lifford ↔ Gibbs, **E**nergy minimization

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT602 - ROBOTIC VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Object Detection** is a fundamental computer vision task that involves simultaneously **localizing** (determining *where* an object is) and **classifying** (determining *what* the object is) instances of semantic objects of predefined classes within a digital image or a video frame. Formally, for a given input image $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, an object detection model $\mathcal{F}$ produces a set of detections:

$$\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{I}) = \{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$

where $b_i = (x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)$ represents the **bounding box** coordinates (center and dimensions), $c_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ is the **class label** from $K$ predefined categories, and $s_i \in [0, 1]$ is the **confidence score** denoting the model's certainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Object detection is treated as a **dual-task** problem: *Classification* (What?) + *Localization* (Where?). A robot must perform both tasks to interact meaningfully with its environment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **security guard in a crowded airport terminal** asked to identify all suspicious luggage. You must do two things simultaneously:

1. **Point to each bag** (this is *localization* — giving a bounding box around it).
2. **Tell what kind of bag it is** (this is *classification* — labeling it as "backpack", "suitcase", or "handbag").
3. **State how confident you are** (this is the *confidence score* — saying "I'm 92% sure it's a suitcase").

Object detection does exactly this on a pixel grid. The "bounding box" is the rectangular region you point at, the "class label" is your identification, and the "confidence" is your certainty. Modern robotic vision systems perform this for **dozens of object categories in milliseconds**, allowing autonomous vehicles, manipulators, and drones to perceive their environment in real-time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Image Classification:**
> - **Classification** → "There is a *car* in this image." (1 label for the whole image)
> - **Object Detection** → "There are 3 *cars* in this image, located at these coordinates." (multiple labels + locations)
> - **Semantic Segmentation** → "Every pixel that belongs to a *car* is colored blue." (pixel-level mask)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Object Detection

The following standardized metrics are universally used (and frequently tested in KTU examinations):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Intersection over Union | $\text{IoU}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth box |
| Confidence Score | $s$ | $[0, 1]$ | Model's certainty for a detection |
| Mean Average Precision | $\text{mAP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Holistic performance metric |
| Frames Per Second | $\text{FPS}$ | $> 0$ | Real-time processing speed |
| Threshold | $\tau$ | $[0, 1]$ | Decision cutoff (commonly $\tau = 0.5$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IoU threshold** $\tau_{\text{IoU}} = 0.5$ is the de-facto standard in the **PASCAL VOC** challenge, while **COCO** uses multiple thresholds from $0.5$ to $0.95$ in steps of $0.05$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Object Detection Algorithms

Object detection methodologies are broadly classified into two paradigms:

**A. Traditional (Hand-crafted Feature) Methods**
- **Viola-Jones Face Detector (2001):** Uses Haar-like features and AdaBoost cascade classifiers. Limited to frontal faces but extremely fast.
- **HOG + SVM (2005):** Histogram of Oriented Gradients descriptors fed to a Support Vector Machine. Used in classical pedestrian detection (Dalal-Triggs).
- **Deformable Parts Models - DPM (2008):** Models objects as collections of parts with flexible spatial arrangements. Won VOC 2007/2008/2009.

**B. Deep Learning Based Methods**
- **Two-Stage Detectors:** Region proposal followed by classification (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN).
- **One-Stage Detectors:** Single-shot regression-based prediction (YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet).
- **Transformer-Based Detectors:** DETR, Deformable DETR (using attention mechanisms).

### 2.2 Core Operational Steps (Generic Pipeline)

A standard object detection pipeline executes the following steps:

1. **Input Preprocessing:** Resize, normalize, and augment the input image (random crops, flips, color jitter).
2. **Feature Extraction:** Pass image through a **Convolutional Backbone** (e.g., ResNet-50, VGG-16, MobileNet) to obtain a feature map $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W' \times C}$.
3. **Region Proposal (Two-Stage only):** Generate candidate object locations, e.g., via a **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** in Faster R-CNN.
4. **Classification & Regression Heads:** For each candidate, predict class probabilities $p(c \vert b)$ and refine box coordinates $\Delta b$.
5. **Post-Processing:** Apply **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** to eliminate duplicate detections.
6. **Output Rendering:** Draw surviving bounding boxes with labels and confidence on the original image.

### 2.3 Intersection over Union (IoU) — The Foundational Metric

The **IoU** (also called the **Jaccard Index**) quantifies the overlap between a predicted bounding box $B_p$ and the ground-truth box $B_{gt}$:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}$$

- $\text{IoU} = 1 \Rightarrow$ perfect overlap
- $\text{IoU} = 0 \Rightarrow$ no overlap
- A detection is considered a **True Positive (TP)** if $\text{IoU} \geq \tau_{\text{IoU}}$ (typically $\tau = 0.5$).

### 2.4 Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Algorithm

NMS removes redundant overlapping detections of the same object:

1. Sort all detections by confidence score $s_i$ in descending order.
2. Select the detection with the **highest score** and add it to the output list $\mathcal{D}_{\text{keep}}$.
3. Compute IoU between this top-scoring box and all remaining boxes.
4. **Suppress (remove)** any box with $\text{IoU} > \tau_{\text{NMS}}$ (typically $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$).
5. Repeat from step 2 until the list is empty.

### 2.5 Mean Average Precision (mAP) — The Gold Standard Metric

For each class $c$:
- Compute the **Precision-Recall (PR) curve** by varying the confidence threshold.
- $\text{AP}_c$ = area under the PR curve (using 11-point or all-point interpolation).
- $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{c=1}^{K} \text{AP}_c$

For COCO: $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{\tau \in \{0.5, 0.55, \dots, 0.95\}} \text{mAP}_{\tau}$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IoU** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Bounding box areas in pixels | Evaluates localization quality in robotics |
| **Precision** | $\text{P} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | TP = True Positives, FP = False Positives | Measures detection reliability |
| **Recall** | $\text{R} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | FN = False Negatives | Measures detection completeness |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | Balanced performance metric |
| **Average Precision (11-pt)** | $\text{AP} = \dfrac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \dots, 1\}} P(r)$ | Sampled recall points | PASCAL VOC metric |
| **YOLO Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{box}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{obj}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}$ | $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ (weight) | Single-stage detector training |
| **Anchor Box Aspect Ratio** | $a = \dfrac{w_a}{h_a}$ | $w_a, h_a$ = anchor width, height | Predefined box shapes |
| **NMS Threshold** | $\tau_{\text{NMS}} \in [0.3, 0.7]$ | Typically $0.5$ | Filters duplicate boxes |
| **Bounding Box Regression** | $b_x = \sigma(t_x) + c_x$ | $t_x$ = predicted offset | Decoding box coordinates |
| **Feature Map Size** | $H' = \dfrac{H}{s}, \quad W' = \dfrac{W}{s}$ | $s$ = stride (e.g., 32, 16, 8) | CNN backbone output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these metrics matter in robotics:** A robotic arm in a warehouse must detect *every* box (high recall) and *only* the correct ones (high precision). An autonomous vehicle cannot afford false negatives (missing a pedestrian) or false positives (phantom braking).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Detect pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signs, lane markings.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Bin-picking, quality inspection, assembly verification.
- **Medical Robotics:** Tumor detection in MRI/CT scans for surgical planning.
- **Agricultural Robots:** Fruit detection, weed identification, crop monitoring.
- **Surveillance & Security:** Intrusion detection, crowd density estimation.
- **Drones:** Obstacle avoidance, search and rescue, package delivery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Intersection over Union (IoU)

Given two bounding boxes in 2D image space:
- Predicted box: $B_p = (x_{p}^{\min}, y_{p}^{\min}, x_{p}^{\max}, y_{p}^{\max})$
- Ground-truth box: $B_{gt} = (x_{gt}^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})$

**Step 1: Compute the intersection rectangle coordinates.**

The overlapping region's top-left corner is the maximum of the top-left corners of both boxes:

$$x_{\text{inter}}^{\min} = \max(x_{p}^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\min})$$

$$y_{\text{inter}}^{\min} = \max(y_{p}^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min})$$

The overlapping region's bottom-right corner is the minimum of the bottom-right corners of both boxes:

$$x_{\text{inter}}^{\max} = \min(x_{p}^{\max}, x_{gt}^{\max})$$

$$y_{\text{inter}}^{\max} = \min(y_{p}^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})$$

**Step 2: Compute the intersection area.**

If the boxes do not overlap, the intersection has zero area. The width and height of the intersection rectangle are:

$$w_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, \; x_{\text{inter}}^{\max} - x_{\text{inter}}^{\min})$$

$$h_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, \; y_{\text{inter}}^{\max} - y_{\text{inter}}^{\min})$$

The intersection area is therefore:

$$A_{\text{inter}} = w_{\text{inter}} \times h_{\text{inter}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the union area using the inclusion-exclusion principle.**

$$A_{p} = (x_{p}^{\max} - x_{p}^{\min}) \times (y_{p}^{\max} - y_{p}^{\min})$$

$$A_{gt} = (x_{gt}^{\max} - x_{gt}^{\min}) \times (y_{gt}^{\max} - y_{gt}^{\min})$$

The union area is the sum of both areas minus their intersection (since the intersection was counted twice):

$$A_{\text{union}} = A_{p} + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}$$

**Step 4: Compute IoU as the ratio.**

$$\boxed{\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{p} + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **guaranteed to appear** in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize all four steps and the $\max(0, \cdot)$ safeguard for non-overlapping cases.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the YOLO Loss Function

The YOLO (You Only Look Once) loss function combines **three components** evaluated over an $S \times S$ grid on the image:

$$\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \right]$$

$$+ \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right]$$

$$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$

$$+ \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$

$$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$

**Step-by-step explanation of each term:**

**Term 1 — Center Coordinate Loss (Localization):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{xy}} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \right]$$
- Penalizes error in the predicted center $(x_i, y_i)$ vs. ground truth $(\hat{x}_i, \hat{y}_i)$.
- $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} = 1$ if an object exists in cell $i$, predictor $j$. Otherwise $0$.
- $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ is a weighting constant that up-weights coordinate loss.

**Term 2 — Width-Height Loss (Square-root trick):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{wh}} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right]$$
- The square root is taken because large boxes should not dominate the loss — we care about *relative* error, not absolute pixel error.
- A $10$-pixel error matters more for a $20 \times 20$ box than for a $200 \times 200$ box.

**Term 3 — Object Confidence Loss:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{obj}} = \sum \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$
- $C_i$ is the predicted confidence (IoU between predicted box and any ground truth).
- $\hat{C}_i$ is the ground-truth confidence (typically $1$ if object is present).

**Term 4 — No-Object Confidence Loss:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{noobj}} = \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$
- $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ down-weights this term because most cells contain no object.
- Prevents the network from predicting high confidence everywhere.

**Term 5 — Classification Loss:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}} = \sum \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$
- Standard squared-error between predicted class probability $p_i(c)$ and one-hot ground truth $\hat{p}_i(c)$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Computing mAP from a Confusion Scenario

**Problem:** A robot's object detector produces the following results on a validation set of $50$ images containing $30$ "cup" objects:

| Confidence (sorted) | Predicted Class | Ground Truth | IoU | TP/FP |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.98 | cup | cup | 0.92 | TP |
| 0.95 | cup | cup | 0.88 | TP |
| 0.91 | cup | cup | 0.45 | FP (IoU < 0.5) |
| 0.85 | cup | cup | 0.81 | TP |
| 0.76 | cup | cup | 0.79 | TP |
| 0.62 | cup | cup | 0.55 | TP |
| 0.55 | cup | (none) | — | FP |
| 0.41 | cup | cup | 0.71 | TP |

After NMS, suppose $7$ detections remain. Compute Precision, Recall, and AP (11-point).

**Step 1: Count TP and FP cumulatively.**

| Rank | Conf | TP/FP | Cumulative TP | Cumulative FP | Precision $\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ | Recall $\frac{\text{TP}}{30}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.98 | TP | 1 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.033 |
| 2 | 0.95 | TP | 2 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.067 |
| 3 | 0.91 | FP | 2 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.067 |
| 4 | 0.85 | TP | 3 | 1 | 0.750 | 0.100 |
| 5 | 0.76 | TP | 4 | 1 | 0.800 | 0.133 |
| 6 | 0.62 | TP | 5 | 1 | 0.833 | 0.167 |
| 7 | 0.55 | FP | 5 | 2 | 0.714 | 0.167 |
| 8 | 0.41 | TP | 6 | 2 | 0.750 | 0.200 |

**Step 2: Apply 11-point interpolation for AP.**

For each recall level $r \in \{0, 0.1, 0.2, \dots, 1.0\}$, take the maximum precision at any recall $\geq r$:

| Recall $r$ | Max Precision $P(r)$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 1.000 |
| 0.1 | 0.750 |
| 0.2 | 0.750 |
| 0.3 | 0.000 (no detections with recall $\geq 0.3$ yet) |
| ... | ... |
| 1.0 | 0.000 |

$$\text{AP} = \frac{1}{11} \sum_{r} P(r) = \frac{1.000 + 0.750 + 0.750 + 0 + \dots + 0}{11}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice how FP detections (Rank 3 and Rank 7) *decrease* precision without *increasing* recall. This is why high-confidence thresholds favor precision, while low thresholds favor recall. The PR curve captures this trade-off geometrically.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Two-Stage vs One-Stage Detectors

| Aspect | Two-Stage (e.g., Faster R-CNN) | One-Stage (e.g., YOLO, SSD) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pipeline** | Region Proposal $\to$ Classification | Direct Regression |
| **Speed (FPS)** | $\sim 7$ (Faster R-CNN) | $30 - 155$ (YOLOv8) |
| **Accuracy (mAP)** | High (especially for small objects) | Slightly lower historically, but closing the gap |
| **Training** | Multi-stage, more complex | End-to-end, simpler |
| **Best for Robotics** | Accuracy-critical (medical, surgical) | Real-time (autonomous driving, drones) |
| **Key Innovation** | Region Proposal Network (RPN) | Unified grid-based prediction |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Object Detection Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Preprocessing: Resize and Normalize]
    B --> C[CNN Backbone: ResNet or VGG]
    C --> D[Feature Map F]
    D --> E{Detector Type}
    E -->|Two-Stage| F1[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    E -->|One-Stage| F2[Grid Based Regression Head]
    F1 --> G1[RoI Pooling]
    F2 --> G2[Anchor Box Predictions]
    G1 --> H1[Classification and Box Regression]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I[Raw Detections]
    I --> J[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    J --> K[Final Bounding Boxes with Class Labels]
    K --> L[Robotic Action: Grasp, Navigate, Inspect]
```

### 4.2 Faster R-CNN Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Image 800x800x3] --> B1[Shared CNN Backbone]
    B1 --> C1[Conv Feature Map 50x50x512]
    C1 --> D1[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    D1 --> E1[Proposed RoIs]
    C1 --> F1[RoI Pooling Layer]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[FC Layer 4096]
    G1 --> H1[Classifier Softmax K classes]
    G1 --> I1[BBox Regressor 4K outputs]
    H1 --> J1[Final Detections]
    I1 --> J1
```

### 4.3 YOLO Single-Shot Detection Grid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Input Image 448x448x3] --> B2[CNN Backbone Darknet 53]
    B2 --> C2[Feature Map 7x7x1024]
    C2 --> D2[1x1 Convolution Head]
    D2 --> E2[Output Tensor 7x7x30]
    E2 --> F2[Decode Bounding Boxes per Cell]
    F2 --> G2[Confidence Filtering tau equals 0.5]
    G2 --> H2[Non Maximum Suppression]
    H2 --> I2[Final Bounding Boxes]
```

### 4.4 NMS Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[All Detections B and Scores S] --> B3[Sort by Score Descending]
    B3 --> C3{List Empty?}
    C3 -->|Yes| D3[Return Filtered Detections]
    C3 -->|No| E3[Pick Box with Highest Score b_max]
    E3 --> F3[Add b_max to Output List]
    E3 --> G3[Compute IoU with All Other Boxes]
    G3 --> H3{IoU greater than tau_NMS?}
    H3 -->|Yes| I3[Suppress Box]
    H3 -->|No| J3[Keep Box]
    I3 --> K3[Remove from Candidate List]
    J3 --> K3
    K3 --> C3
```

### 4.5 Bounding Box Parameterization Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Ground Truth Box: cx_gt, cy_gt, w_gt, h_gt] --> B4[Network Predicts: tx, ty, tw, th]
    B4 --> C4[Decoding Equations]
    C4 --> D4[Predicted Center: bx equals sigma of tx plus cx]
    C4 --> E4[Predicted Center: by equals sigma of ty plus cy]
    C4 --> F4[Predicted Width: bw equals pw times exp of tw]
    C4 --> G4[Predicted Height: bh equals ph times exp of th]
    D4 --> H4[Final Bounding Box]
    E4 --> H4
    F4 --> H4
    G4 --> H4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams illustrate the **end-to-end functional flow** of modern object detectors. For a robotic vision exam, drawing a similar high-level block diagram (with at least 4-5 functional blocks and arrows) easily fetches 3-4 marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Object Detection" and distinguish it from "Image Classification". List any two real-world applications of object detection in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Object detection is a computer vision task that involves both **localizing** instances of objects (by drawing bounding boxes) and **classifying** them into predefined categories, in a single inference step.

**Distinction (1 Mark):**
- **Image Classification** outputs a single class label for the *entire* image (e.g., "This image contains a dog").
- **Object Detection** outputs *multiple* bounding boxes, each with its own class label and confidence (e.g., "Dog at [x=120, y=80, w=200, h=180], Cat at [x=350, y=200, w=150, h=160]").

**Applications (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time detection of pedestrians, traffic signs, and other vehicles.
2. **Industrial Robotic Manipulation:** Bin-picking — locating randomly placed objects for a gripper.
3. **Medical Robotics:** Tumor or lesion detection in MRI/CT for surgical planning.
4. **Agricultural Robots:** Detecting ripe fruits for automated harvesting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the role of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) in object detection? State the IoU threshold typically used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of NMS (2 Marks):** Object detectors often produce **multiple overlapping bounding boxes** for the same object, each with slightly different coordinates and confidence scores. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** is a post-processing algorithm that:
1. Sorts all candidate detections by confidence score.
2. Selects the detection with the **highest confidence**.
3. Suppresses (removes) all other detections whose **IoU with the selected box exceeds the NMS threshold**.
4. Iterates until all detections are processed.

This ensures that only **one bounding box per object** is retained in the final output, eliminating redundancy.

**IoU Threshold (1 Mark):** The typical NMS threshold is $\tau_{\text{NMS}} = 0.5$. Detections with $\text{IoU} > 0.5$ with the highest-scoring box are suppressed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Choice 1`

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (7 Marks)**
**Explain the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric used in object detection. Derive the formula for IoU given two bounding boxes $B_p$ and $B_{gt}$. What IoU threshold is conventionally used to classify a detection as a True Positive?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Intersection over Union (IoU), also known as the **Jaccard Index**, is a metric that quantifies the overlap between the predicted bounding box $B_p$ and the ground-truth bounding box $B_{gt}$. It is defined as the ratio of the intersection area to the union area.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Let $B_p = (x_{p}^{\min}, y_{p}^{\min}, x_{p}^{\max}, y_{p}^{\max})$ and $B_{gt} = (x_{gt}^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})$.

**Step 1:** Compute the coordinates of the intersection rectangle:

$$x_{\text{inter}}^{\min} = \max(x_{p}^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\min}), \quad y_{\text{inter}}^{\min} = \max(y_{p}^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min})$$

$$x_{\text{inter}}^{\max} = \min(x_{p}^{\max}, x_{gt}^{\max}), \quad y_{\text{inter}}^{\max} = \min(y_{p}^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})$$

**Step 2:** Intersection area (with safeguard for no overlap):

$$A_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, x_{\text{inter}}^{\max} - x_{\text{inter}}^{\min}) \times \max(0, y_{\text{inter}}^{\max} - y_{\text{inter}}^{\min})$$

**Step 3:** Union area via inclusion-exclusion:

$$A_{\text{union}} = A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}$$

**Step 4:** IoU:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}}$$

**Threshold (1 Mark):** A detection is classified as a **True Positive (TP)** if $\text{IoU} \geq \tau$, where the **conventional threshold is $\tau = 0.5$** (PASCAL VOC standard). COCO uses averaged mAP over multiple thresholds from $0.5$ to $0.95$.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - June 2024] (7 Marks)**
**With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the Faster R-CNN object detector. Highlight the role of the Region Proposal Network (RPN).**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Architecture Description (4 Marks):** Faster R-CNN consists of four major components:

1. **Shared Convolutional Backbone (1 Mark):** A pre-trained CNN (e.g., ResNet-50 or VGG-16) extracts a high-level convolutional feature map from the input image. This feature map is shared between the RPN and the detector head, reducing computation.

2. **Region Proposal Network (RPN) — 1.5 Marks:** The RPN is a small convolutional network that slides over the feature map and outputs a set of rectangular **object proposals** with objectness scores. It uses **anchor boxes** of multiple scales and aspect ratios as reference templates. For each anchor, the RPN predicts:
   - An **objectness score** (foreground vs. background).
   - **Bounding box deltas** to refine the anchor.
   
   The RPN is trained with a multi-task loss combining classification and regression.

3. **RoI Pooling Layer (1 Mark):** Converts the variable-sized proposed regions into **fixed-size feature maps** (e.g., $7 \times 7$) by dividing each region into a grid and performing max-pooling within each bin.

4. **Detection Head (0.5 Marks):** Fully-connected layers branch into:
   - A **classifier** (softmax over $K+1$ classes including background).
   - A **bounding box regressor** that outputs refined $4$-D box coordinates per class.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** The student must draw a block diagram similar to Section 4.2 with these blocks: Input Image $\to$ CNN Backbone $\to$ Feature Map $\to$ RPN $\to$ RoI Pooling $\to$ FC Layers $\to$ Classifier + Bbox Regressor $\to$ Final Detections. Arrows must clearly show the shared backbone feeding both RPN and RoI Pooling.

**Role of RPN (1 Mark — included in point 2 above):** The RPN **eliminates the need for external region proposal algorithms** (like Selective Search used in the original R-CNN), reducing proposal generation time from seconds to milliseconds, thus enabling near real-time detection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Choice 2` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] (7 Marks)**
**Describe the YOLO (You Only Look Once) object detection algorithm. Explain how it differs from traditional two-stage detectors and list its advantages for robotic vision applications.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**YOLO Algorithm (3 Marks):** YOLO is a **single-stage (one-shot) object detector** that frames detection as a single regression problem. The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (typically $7 \times 7$ in YOLOv1). Each grid cell is responsible for predicting:
- $B$ bounding boxes (with $x, y, w, h$ and confidence).
- Class probabilities $p(c)$ for $C$ classes.
- The output is a single tensor of size $S \times S \times (5B + C)$.

A single CNN (e.g., Darknet-53 backbone) processes the entire image in one forward pass, producing all detections simultaneously.

**Difference from Two-Stage Detectors (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | YOLO (One-Stage) | Faster R-CNN (Two-Stage) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pipeline | Single forward pass | Region proposal + classification |
| Speed | Real-time ($30 - 155$ FPS) | Slower ($\sim 7$ FPS) |
| Accuracy | Slightly lower on small objects | Higher on small/dense objects |
| Global Reasoning | Yes — sees entire image | Limited to proposed regions |

**Advantages for Robotic Vision (2 Marks):**
1. **Real-Time Performance:** Critical for autonomous vehicles and drones that cannot tolerate high latency.
2. **End-to-End Learning:** A single differentiable loss function, simpler training pipeline.
3. **Fewer Background Errors:** Because YOLO reasons about the full image, it is less likely to confuse background patches for objects (compared to region-based methods).
4. **Compact Models:** Variants like YOLOv8-Nano run on edge devices (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi) used in mobile robots.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - June 2023] (7 Marks)**
**What is Mean Average Precision (mAP)? Explain with a numerical example how mAP is computed for an object detector across multiple classes.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** is the primary evaluation metric for object detection. It is the mean of the **Average Precision (AP)** scores computed for each class separately. For a dataset with $K$ classes:

$$\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{c=1}^{K} \text{AP}_c$$

**Numerical Example (4 Marks):** Consider a 3-class detection problem (cup, bottle, book) with the following AP values computed from Precision-Recall curves:

| Class | AP |
| :--- | :---: |
| Cup | $0.82$ |
| Bottle | $0.76$ |
| Book | $0.91$ |

Then:
$$\text{mAP} = \frac{0.82 + 0.76 + 0.91}{3} = \frac{2.49}{3} = 0.83$$

**Steps to Compute AP per Class (1 Mark):**
1. Sort all detections of that class by confidence score (descending).
2. Compute cumulative TP and FP at each rank.
3. Calculate Precision $P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}}$ and Recall $R = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{Total GT}}$.
4. Plot $P$ vs. $R$ to obtain the PR curve.
5. AP = area under the PR curve (using 11-point or all-point interpolation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing mAP with AP:** AP is *per-class*; mAP is the *mean over all classes*. Students often use the terms interchangeably and lose 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the IoU threshold:** A detection is a TP only if $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ **and** the class is correct. Students frequently count class-correct but poorly-localized boxes as TP.
> 3. **Not applying NMS before computing mAP:** Raw detector output has many duplicates — always apply NMS first.
> 4. **Confusing IoU with NMS threshold:** Both are typically $0.5$, but they serve *different* purposes. IoU threshold $\to$ TP classification. NMS threshold $\to$ duplicate removal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Object Detection**

- [x] **Object Detection = Localization + Classification** (the dual-task definition).
- [x] **Output format:** $\{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}$ — bounding box, class, confidence.
- [x] **IoU Formula:** $\dfrac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}}$ with $\max(0, \cdot)$ safeguard.
- [x] **TP Threshold:** $\tau_{\text{IoU}} = 0.5$ (PASCAL VOC), $0.5$ to $0.95$ averaged (COCO).
- [x] **NMS Algorithm:** Sort $\to$ Pick max $\to$ Suppress IoU > $\tau_{\text{NMS}}$ $\to$ Repeat.
- [x] **mAP Definition:** Mean of per-class Average Precision values.
- [x] **AP Computation:** Area under Precision-Recall curve (interpolated).
- [x] **Two-Stage Detectors:** R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN (RPN replaces Selective Search).
- [x] **One-Stage Detectors:** YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet (single forward pass, real-time).
- [x] **YOLO Loss:** 5 terms — $\mathcal{L}_{xy} + \mathcal{L}_{wh} + \mathcal{L}_{obj} + \mathcal{L}_{noobj} + \mathcal{L}_{cls}$ with weights $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ and $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$.
- [x] **Anchor Boxes:** Predefined reference boxes of multiple scales and aspect ratios.
- [x] **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN):** Detects objects at multiple scales.
- [x] **Robotic Applications:** Autonomous driving, bin-picking, medical imaging, agriculture, surveillance.
- [x] **Backbones:** ResNet, VGG, MobileNet, Darknet, EfficientNet.
- [x] **Key Difference:** Classification $\to$ 1 label. Detection $\to$ many (box, class, score). Segmentation $\to$ pixel mask.
- [x] **Standard Test Datasets:** PASCAL VOC (20 classes), MS COCO (80 classes), ImageNet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** (at least 4-5 labeled blocks with arrows) for any detector architecture question. KTU examiners award 2-3 marks for a clean, well-labeled diagram even if the written explanation is incomplete.
